title: 3  American lobster
section: 4.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
4.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
The Northwest Atlantic has been warming at an exceptionally fast rate (Saba et al. 2016), which has contributed to declines in fecundity and population size in Southern New England (Goldstein, Zarrella-Smith, and Pugh 2022) while the fishery in the central Gulf of Maine has increased dramatically (Le Bris et al. 2018). Temperatures have increased in the lobster’s inshore range, and evidence suggests that the productivity of the stock is severely compromised with increased thermal stress and disease (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC) 2025). The assemblage of predators of American lobster has shifted with warming conditions in the Gulf of Maine, with benthic and demersal predators, such as black sea bass (Centropristis striata) and spiny dogfish (Squalus acanthias) becoming increasingly present throughout the lobster’s range, taking the place of traditional predators, such as Atlantic cod (Gadus morhua). The effects of invasive and range-expanding species may also lead to greater predation risk for juvenile lobsters (Goldstein et al. 2025).

title: 3  American lobster
text:
4.8 Life History Synopsis:
American lobsters inhabit the coastal and oceanic waters of the Atlantic Ocean from Labrador to North Carolina. They are widely distributed within most of the coastal waters in the Northeast U.S. with some major offshore concentrations along the outer edge of the Continental Shelf and upper slope between Georges Bank and Delaware Bay (Mackenzie and Moring 1985). Spawning takes place during a 4 month period from late May through much of September, when females leave their solitary shelters about 7 days before molting to share the shelter of a dominant male (Factor 1995) (Mackenzie and Moring 1985). Lobster larvae are planktonic for 6-8 weeks (Factor 1995) and travel from surface waters offshore to inshore areas of Southern New England and the Gulf of Maine. Larval distribution is affected by the distribution of spawning females and by surface current velocity and direction, temperature, salinity, light intensity, and hydrostatic pressure (Katz, Cobb, and Spaulding 1994) (Harding and Trites 1988) (Hudson and Fradette 1993) (Mackenzie and Moring 1985). Larval survival is likely affected by pollution of the marine environment by human activity, especially coastal waters (Connor 1972). There is an inverse relationship between temperature and the time from hatching to the postlarval stage (when they settle). Larvae take as few as 11 days at 22°C and as many as 54 days at 10°C to reach the postlarval stage. It is speculated that thermoclines are a negative cue for larvae/post larvae settlement (Factor 1995) (Templeman 1936). Juvenile lobsters utilize shelters (rocks and vegetation) in the benthic habitat, and may also burrow in mud (Wahle and Steneck 1991). Juveniles are omnivorous but also scavenge for food. Much of their diet consists of bottom invertebrates, crabs, urchins, mussels, polychaetes, periwinkles, and sea stars, but they may also consume fish and plants (Lawton 1987). Adult lobsters are also considered omnivores and scavengers . However, their diet is dominated by mollusks (especially mussels), echinoderms, and polychaetes (Elner and Campbell 1987). Juveniles and adults prefer bottom water temperatures of 8-14°C (Mackenzie and Moring 1985). Inshore, adult lobsters appear to have a limited home range of about 2.2 km, however offshore lobsters tend to migrate greater distances (up to 345 km in 71 days) (Mackenzie and Moring 1985). American lobster are managed by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission as three stock units based on life history differences: Gulf of Maine, Georges Bank, and southern New England (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC) 2025).

title: 4  Atlantic cod
section: 5.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
5.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Recent warming in the Gulf of Maine relating to a northward shift in the Gulf Stream and changes in the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation and Pacific Decadal Oscillation led to reduced Atlantic cod recruitment and increased mortality (Pershing et al. 2015). Rising temperatures may affect Atlantic cod spawning locations and timing (Behan et al. 2023). Drinkwater (2005) suggested Atlantic cod spawning grounds will experience a shift northward, with spawning timing occurring later in the season as a response to warming ocean temperatures. Climate change may also increase in natural mortality in adult Atlantic cod due to declines in fish condition (Behan et al. 2023). Drinkwater (2005) predicted large-scale declines in Atlantic cod in the Gulf of Maine if bottom temperatures increase above 12°C, either through changes in distribution to colder waters or natural mortality. Reduced fitness in Atlantic cod adults from climate impacts may result in lower quality eggs (Klein, Smith, and Kritzer 2016); however, there may be some capacity to adapt to slower rates of warming (Langan et al. 2020).

title: 4  Atlantic cod
text:
5.8 Life History Synopsis:
Cod in the Northwest Atlantic range from Greenland to Cape Hatteras, NC, with a majority of biomass in the Gulf of Maine and on Georges Bank (Behan et al. 2023). Spawning timing varies by location - later in the year with increasing latitude - although peaks generally occur in the winter and spring (Lough 2004) in water temperatures between 5-7°C. In spring, Atlantic cod spawning matched the variation in peak abundance of Calanus finmarchicus. In fall, cod spawned in some, but not all, areas where Calanus were abundant. This is consistent with the hypothesis that cod spawning is coupled to copepod production (Brander and Hurley 1992). Atlantic cod eggs are pelagic and hatch after 8-60 days, varying with temperature (Hardy 1978). Recruitment success increases when dispersion of larvae by currents is reduced (Cong, Sheng, and Thompson 1996). On Georges Bank, a clockwise gyre circulation pattern tends to retain and isolate eggs and larvae (Lough and Bolz 1989). Transformation from larvae to the juvenile stage occurs at sizes greater than 20 mm, where they then move to the bottom (Lough 2004). Early juveniles are dependent on gravel substrates or grass beds in nearshore nurseries to favor survival through predator avoidance and increased food availability (Lough and Bolz 1989). In the Gulf of Maine, juveniles prey on mollusks and other invertebrates, while adults consume a greater proportion of fish including herring, redfish, mackerel, and other cod (Bowman 1975). Adult cod have few predators, other than large sharks and seals (Neuenhoff et al. 2019). Recent modeling studies illustrate growing populations of grey seals, harp seals, and dogfish are a major barrier to cod population recovery (Behan et al. 2023). Adults in the Newfoundland stock migrate, leaving overwintering areas in deep oceanic waters and follow tongues of deep, relatively warm, oceanic waters (“highways”) across the shelf to summer feeding areas nearshore. They then move northward along the Newfoundland coast in late summer, and eventually return to wintering areas (Lough 2004). Previous research suggested that in the middle part of their range, Atlantic cod only undertake minor seasonal movements in reaction to changing temperatures. However, more recent research suggests that there are inshore/offshore migrations that correspond to spawning and feeding activity in the Gulf of Maine (Zemeckis et al. 2017). As of 2025, Atlantic cod are divided into 4 stock units (Western & Eastern Gulf Maine, Georges Bank, and Southern New England) based on spawning locations, larval transport pathways, and genetic markers (New England Fishery Management Council (NEFMC) 2025). All stocks are managed under the New England Fishery Management Council’s Northeast Multispecies Fishery Management Plan (Klein-MacPhee 2012). The stock is overfished and overfishing is occurring in the Gulf of Maine (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2021).

title: 5  Atlantic croaker
section: 6.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
6.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
As a sub-tropical species, Atlantic croaker may experience both positive and negative effects on their abundance and distribution as a result of climate change. Interannual variability in croaker abundance may be climate related, with colder winters causing increased mortality in overwintering young-of-the-year (Lankford Jr and Targett 2001). Warm winters, however, may result in increased abundances, or “outbursts” of juveniles in the spring that contribute to expansion of the population’s distribution (Hare and Able 2007). Diamond, Murphy, and Rose (2013) modeled Atlantic croaker abundance in the Mid-Atlantic with global climate change projections and found increased croaker abundance with lower inter-annual variability under global climate change. Positive responses in sensitivity analyses stemmed from warmer temperatures, and negative responses were due to rising variability in salinity and increases in offshore transport and sea level rise (Diamond, Murphy, and Rose 2013). Common juvenile habitat types (submerged aquatic vegetation, salt marshes, and intertidal habitats) are highly vulnerable to climate change (Guyant et al. 2022).

title: 5  Atlantic croaker
text:
6.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic croaker are a demersal species found from the Gulf of Maine to Argentina. Along the United States Atlantic coast, they are most abundant from Chesapeake Bay to northern Florida (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2020). Atlantic croaker spawn from July - December in inshore shelf and estuarine waters between Delaware Bay and Cape Hatteras, peaking in August - October near Chesapeake Bay (Able and Fahay 1998). Their name comes from the croaking noises the fish make during spawning by vibrating their abdominal muscles against their swim bladder (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2020). After 30-90 days post-hatch Atlantic croaker larvae are driven inshore by winds (Thorrold, Jones, and Campana 1997) where they migrate to estuarine habitats (Sempsrott et al. 2017). Initial fall recruitment of croaker depends on fall continental shelf winds to provide transport into Chesapeake Bay (Norcross 1991). Recruitment to specific estuaries is influenced by tidal activity and currents, such that eggs and larvae are dependent on transport to suitable habitats for survival (Sempsrott et al. 2017). Atlantic croaker larvae are selective feeders, as they eat fewer taxa of suitable size than are present in ambient plankton (Govoni et al. 1986). Juvenile croaker are highly dependent on marine, estuarine, and riverine mud and sand substrates, as well as native salt marsh habitats (Guyant et al. 2022). The major prey of young-of-the-year Atlantic croaker are polychaetes, copepods, and mysids. Detritus is also a major component of the juvenile diet (Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission 2010) (Sink 2011). Adult Atlantic croaker perform seasonal migrations, moving northward and inshore to estuaries and bays in the spring - fall and southward and offshore to shelf waters in the winter (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2020). They reach maturity by 2 years of age and can live up to 17 years, although lifespans are commonly below 10 years (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2020). Adult Atlantic croaker are opportunistic feeders and are known to consume polychaetes, anchovies, mysid shrimp, amphipods, fishes and crabs, and detritus in Chesapeake Bay (Nye, Loewensteiner, and Miller 2011). Atlantic croaker are managed under Amendment 1 to the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission’s Interstate Fishery Management Plan for Atlantic Croaker (2005) and Addenda I, II, and III (2011, 2014, 2020). (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2020). The most recent benchmark stock assessment was completed in 2017, where the current stock status could not be determined because the assessment results were sensitive to certain modeling assumptions, but any further depletion in the stock was unlikely (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2017).

title: 6  Atlantic halibut
section: 7.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
7.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Nye et al. (2009) observed a northward shift in Atlantic halibut distribution and an increase in the depth of occurrence on the Northeast U.S. continental shelf. The species’ thermal habitat has widened, likely contributing to its range expansion (Shackell et al. 2022), and biomass and landings in Canadian waters have thus increased greatly since the early 2000s (Trzcinski and Bowen 2016). Shackell et al. (2022) calculated growth rates from tagging studies and found faster growth in Atlantic halibut in warmer, southern waters. From 1990 to 2018, thermal habitat rose by 11.6% (+/- 7.35%) and the number of degree days where Atlantic halibut are experiencing growth increased by 13.5 °C days (+/- 7.86 °C days) (Czich et al. 2023). Continued warming is likely to increase abundance in the northern end of the species’ range; however elevated CO2 levels and rising temperatures may lead to oxidative stress (Almroth et al. 2019).

title: 6  Atlantic halibut
text:
7.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic halibut are found from the Labrador Shelf in the North to Virginia, although they are rare south of Long Island (Cargnelli, Griesbach, and Morse 1999). Adult halibut spawn from late fall to late spring in deep continental shelf waters, with presumed spawning peaks occurring at 2 - 9 day intervals between October and January (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002) (Armsworthy, Trzcinski, and Campana 2014). Eggs hatch in 13-20 days depending on the temperature (Blaxter et al. 1983); larvae have been found in the plankton as much as a year after spawning (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). Depending on current speeds and larval duration, eggs and larvae may drift anywhere from 300-500 km. Tagging studies of spawning individuals suggest that larvae can be transported into the Gulf of St Lawrence, which may provide nursery habitats (Armsworthy, Trzcinski, and Campana 2014). Juveniles are most common in shallow water from 20-60 m in nursery areas on the shelf (Cargnelli, Griesbach, and Morse 1999). Juvenile halibut up to 30 cm in length consume invertebrates, mostly annelid worms and crustaceans; they then feed on fish and invertebrates from 30-80 cm in length, and then almost exclusively on fish as they grow larger (Scott and Scott 1988) (Bowman et al. 2000). Adults normally inhabit benthic waters from 2-15°C, but occupy the entire water column to feed and spawn (Cargnelli, Griesbach, and Morse 1999) (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002) (Armsworthy, Trzcinski, and Campana 2014). In the Gulf of Maine, tagged individuals have been observed conducting both shore and long distance migrations, as well as being resident year-round (Shackell et al. 2022). Age at maturity ranges from 7 - 13 years (females) and 5 - 12 years (males), and their maximum age is between 38-50 years (Armsworthy, Trzcinski, and Campana 2014). Atlantic halibut is currently managed by the New England Fishery Management Council under the Northeast Multispecies Fishery Management Plan. The most recent management track assessment was completed in 2024, where stock status could not be determined analytically due to a lack of biological reference points (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2024).

title: 7  Atlantic herring
section: 8.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
8.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
A number of Atlantic herring life history parameters are linked to drivers of climate change. Climate-related mismatches between Atlantic herring larval feeding and prey abundance have resulted in slower growth and higher mortality (Randall et al. 2022). J. P. Gröger, Kruse, and Rohlf (2010) found that lags in the North Atlantic Oscillation (NAO) and Atlantic Multi-decadal Oscillation (AMO) were the primary drivers of Atlantic herring recruitment. Winter NAO has also indirectly influenced recruitment in multiple herring stocks (Axenrot and Hansson 2003) (Oskarsson and Taggart 2010) (Lynam et al. 2005) and may be used to predict recruitment with lead times up to 5 years (J. P. Gröger, Kruse, and Rohlf 2010). Atlantic herring recruitment is also influenced by larval temperatures, the Gulf Stream Index, and food availability from water mass movement (Molina 2025). Favorable habitat for Atlantic herring in the Northwest Atlantic is currently decreasing with rising temperatures and bottom salinity (Suca et al. 2021), with herring populations in warmer waters responding more negatively to increasing temperatures than those in colder waters (Beaudry-Sylvestre, Benoît, and Hutchings 2024). Herring larvae in the Baltic Sea are facing more heat stress and cardiac dysfunction with an increase in the number of marine heat waves (M. Gröger et al. 2024). Migration and distribution of Atlantic herring is also linked with thermal oceanic fronts between colder, less saline continental shelf water and warmer, more salient slope water (where plankton are also usually more abundant) (Kelly and Moring 1986).

title: 7  Atlantic herring
text:
8.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic herring in the Northwest Atlantic reside from southern Labrador in Canada to Cape Hatteras, NC (Stevenson and Scott 2005). They spawn primarily on Jeffreys Ledge and Georges Bank between July and November, with peaks in August - September in the Gulf of Maine. However, recent research has suggested that the optimal spawning temperature window has shortened over the past 30 years (Kanwit and Libby 2009) (Molina 2025). Early life history development is inversely related to water temperature, with slower growth occurring at higher temperatures (Stevenson and Scott 2005). Eggs hatch after 6-15 days, and larval development time ranges from 4-8 months. Larvae absorb their yolk sac within 2.5 - 6 days, at which point the larvae become pelagic and can be transported away from or retained near spawning habitats, depending on prevailing current systems (Lough and Cohen 1982). Predation of eggs is linked to high mortality. Predators include haddock, cod, mackerel, winter flounder, moon snails, hermit crabs and starfish. Strong haddock recruitment has been linked to low abundances of larval herring (Boyar et al. 1973) (Burbank et al. 2023). Larval herring prey on copepods, with size depending on the size of the larvae (Sherman and Honey 1971). Juveniles form large schools in coastal waters (i.e. estuaries and embayments) throughout the Gulf of Maine in the spring and summer, and overwinter in deep bays or offshore (Stevenson and Scott 2005). Juveniles and adults are planktivorous and opportunistic feeders. As they grow and the size of their jaws increases, they consume larger organisms. Their diet therefore varies with season, their age and size, and location. (Stevenson and Scott 2005) (Sherman and Perkins 1971). A lack of availability of preferred prey in adults (due to strong mixing in the water column) can lead to suboptimal feeding and slow growth resulting in increased mortality from starvation and predation (Stevenson and Scott 2005). Adult herring make extensive seasonal migrations between summer spawning grounds on Georges Bank and in the Gulf of Maine and overwintering areas in southern New England and the Mid-Atlantic region (Stevenson and Scott 2005). There are two major spawning stocks in the Northwest Atlantic: the Gulf of Maine and Georges Bank/Nantucket Shoals. Stocks are separated based on larval distribution patterns, spawning times/locations, and growth rates (Iles and Sinclair 1982) (Boyar et al. 1973) (Anthony and Waring 1980). Both stocks are currently assessed in the U.S. as a single coastal stock complex managed by the New England Fishery Management Council. As of the most recent assessment (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2024), the stock is overfished but not experiencing overfishing based on the biological reference points for spawning stock biomass (SSB) and fishing mortality (F).

title: 8  Atlantic mackerel
section: 9.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
9.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Atlantic mackerel are sensitive to temperature changes, particularly temperatures greater than 5°C (Overholtz, Hare, and Keith 2011). From 1968 to 2008, the distribution of mackerel in the Northwest Atlantic has shifted roughly 250km north and east in addition to changes in depth distribution to more shallow on-shelf waters. These changes in position and depth are known to be correlated with the gradual warming of the Northwest Atlantic (Overholtz, Hare, and Keith 2011). Atlantic mackerel egg distribution has also shifted northeastward as a consequence of warming, depleting egg production in important spawning locations in southern New England (Richardson et al. 2020). Bruge et al. (2016) projected changes in Northeast Atlantic mackerel spawning distribution under future climate scenarios, and found that the spawning center of gravity is expected to shift westward and northward. Mbaye et al. (2019) investigated effects of environmental conditions on Atlantic mackerel spawning habitat in the Gulf of St. Lawrence. Generalized additive model (GAM) results showed that spawning habitat in the Gulf of St. Lawrence could expand and extend to western and southern Newfoundland.

title: 8  Atlantic mackerel
text:
9.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic mackerel in the Northwest Atlantic inhabit cold and temperate continental shelf waters from the Gulf of St Lawrence to Cape Lookout, North Carolina (Studholme 1999). Spawning occurs from April to August in the Northwest Atlantic and progresses from south to north with warming temperatures and following migration patterns (Sette 1943). There are two spawning contingents: the southern contingent spawns from April to July in the Mid-Atlantic Bight and Gulf of Maine, and the northern contingent spawns in the southern Gulf of St. Lawrence in June and July (Berrien 1982) (Morse, Fahay, and Smith 1987). Temperature is a cue for spawning. Spawning does not occur until water has warmed to 8°C with most egg production occurring between 9-14°C (Sette 1943). More recent work has shown that mackerel spawning and migration timing is negatively correlated with sea surface temperature (Jansen and Gislason 2011). Early life stage development happens quickly, with eggs hatching in 4-7.5 hours, depending on temperature (Berrien 1982). Larvae (< 13 mm) occur primarily in offshore waters from Chesapeake Bay to southern Gulf of St. Lawrence (Sette 1943). Larval and juvenile depth varies diurnally and with age (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). Larvae feed primarily on zooplankton and copepods, and larvae > 6.4 mm are known to experience cannibalistic behavior (Castonguay et al. 2008). Juveniles rely on water column habitats, both on the shelf, and in estuaries (Guyant et al. 2022). Juveniles and adults are opportunistic feeders that prey on a wide range of crustaceans, copepods, planktonic organisms, and fishes (Sette 1950). Squid, seabirds and larger piscivores, including Atlantic cod, prey on juvenile mackerel (Studholme 1999). Adults over winter in moderately deep water, 70-200 m, along the continental shelf. After migrating inshore in April-May, they form the two spawning aggregations (Berrien 1982). Predators of adult mackerel include larger fish, sharks, skates, seabirds, and marine mammals (primarily whales and seals) (Studholme 1999). Atlantic mackerel are managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Mackerel, Squid, and Butterfish Fishery Management Plan. As of the 2025 assessment, the stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring, but is rebuilding to target levels (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2025).

title: 9  Atlantic menhaden
section: 10.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
10.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Atlantic menhaden are estuary dependent species, and their habitats may be impacted by future development or trawling and dredging activities (Rogers and Van Den Avyle 1989). Atlantic menhaden distribution is expanding northward, likely to due warmer water temperatures, although other local ecological factors may also influence distribution (Morano 2025). Recruitment of Atlantic menhaden larvae to nursery habitats appears to be driven primarily by larval supply and the oceanographic properties driving transport, as well as nursery habitat quality, rather than specific settlement cues (Quinlan, Blanton, and Werner 1999). Climate change could impact these transport mechanisms or the quality of nursery habitats. In a study modeling menhaden recruitment trends in Chesapeake Bay and Southern New England, a broad-scale climate driver, the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation (AMO) was one of the best predictors of recruitment (Buchheister et al. 2016). Midway et al. (2020) used hierarchical models to determine significant effects of age 1 Atlantic menhaden growth caused by the AMO (negative effect), easterly (positive effect) and northerly (negative effect) wind velocity. Turner (2017) investigated declines in size-at-age for age 3-5 Atlantic menhaden and attributed these size changes to coastal and ocean warming. This study also predicted future reductions in size-at-age as ocean temperatures rise.

title: 9  Atlantic menhaden
text:
10.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic menhaden inhabit coastal and estuarine waters from Nova Scotia to Florida (Rogers and Van Den Avyle 1989). They are a key forage species in the North and South Atlantic, and are consumed by a wide range of predators, including striped bass, bluefish, and weakfish in the North Atlantic (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2025). Menhanden spawning peaks in December - February (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review 2025). Spawning was originally thought to be concentrated off the coast of the Carolinas following the fall migration. However, more recent analysis of larval distributions suggests that spawning occurs throughout the range, as far north as Long Island (Nelson, Ingham, and Schaaf 1977) (Simpson et al. 2017). Menhaden spawning periods coincide with presence of phytoplankton and zooplankton, the dominant food sources of larval menhaden (Rogers and Van Den Avyle 1989). Eggs are buoyant and reliant on transportation by wind and tidal currents towards nursery estuary habitats, where they hatch in a few days (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review 2025). After around 30 days in the larval stage, juveniles move to upper portions of estuaries including river shoals and the heads of small tidal creeks, where they congregate over vegetation in open water (Rogers and Van Den Avyle 1989). Young juvenile menhaden consume individual zooplankton; this reliance on zooplankton decreases as menhaden move from open waters to marshes, although adult menhaden also consume zooplankton (Rogers and Van Den Avyle 1989). Adults prefer temperatures near 18°C and generally remain within the top 100m of the water column (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review 2025). After the winter spawning season, adults migrate inshore and north following spawning grounds in the Carolinas to the Gulf of Maine (Rogers and Van Den Avyle 1989). Atlantic menhaden reach maturity around 2 years of age and a maximum age is listed at 10 years, although individuals older than 6 years of age are rarely caught in the fishery (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review 2025). Menhaden in the Northwest Atlantic are managed as a single stock unit by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. The most recent stock assessment update in 2025 used ecological reference points to determine stock status. As of 2025, the stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2025).

section: 11.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
11.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
The Atlantic sea scallop population in the Northwest Atlantic is subject to climate-related environmental stress from rising temperatures and ocean acidification (Zang et al. 2022). Scallop habitat can be degraded by trawling/dredging activity (Maguire et al. 2002). Scallop feeding behaviors are temporarily impacted by noise pollution associated with offshore wind construction (J’ez’equel et al. 2022). Changes in temperature and food availability are likely the predominant drivers for reduced growth in sea scallops (Cranford et al. 1998). Rheuban et al. (2018) modeled the Atlantic sea scallop fishery under a range of climate and management scenarios and found the possibility of reduced biomass up to 50% by the end of this century. Results of a scope for growth model with increased warming food deficiency indicated a range contraction of suitable sea scallop habitat in the Mid-Atlantic Bight (Zang et al. 2022). A second scope for growth model by Zang et al. (2023) investigated the relationship between sea scallop size and temperature. Under future climate warming, sea scallops exhibited a range contraction and decline in maximum shell height. An integrated assessment model simulating sea scallop population dynamics with oceanographic and socioeconomic influences indicates that sea scallop harvests could experience a substantial decline by 2050 under increased CO2 emissions (with the assumption that ocean acidification decreases recruitment and slows growth and warming increases growth) (Cooley et al. 2015).

title: 10  Atlantic sea scallop
text:
11.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic sea scallops are one of the most economically important fisheries in the Northwest Atlantic, with average annual ex-vessel revenues of over $500 million (Zang et al. 2022). Their habitat ranges from the Gulf of St. Lawrence to Cape Hatteras, North Carolina (Stewart and Arnold 1994) with primary fishing areas in Georges Bank, the Great South Channel, and the Mid-Atlantic Bight (Hart and Rago 2006). After a period of severe overfishing in the 1970s to 1990s, regulations including effort reductions, gear restrictions, and closed areas were introduced to rebuild the stock (Hart and Rago 2006). Spawning occurs in late summer or early fall, although smaller spawning events at other times of the year are possible (Culliney 1974). Gametes from other scallops, agitation, or tides may trigger scallop spawning (Parsons et al. 1992). Larvae reside in the water column for 5-7 weeks (Thouzeau, Robert, and Smith 1991) and circulate with residual currents ranging from 6-25 cm/s (Culliney 1974). Larval exchange is influenced by duration and depth of planktonic drift, gyre strength, and weak cross-isobath flow (Thouzeau, Robert, and Smith 1991). The transition to the benthos of the freeswimming larvae occurs at a size of about 0.25 mm shell height and is accompanied by drastic changes in diet, morphology, and locomotory ability (Bourne 1965). Mean fall temperatures impact the time larvae take to settle (Tremblay et al. 1994). Sea scallop larvae are potentially preyed on by filter feeders and larval and adult planktivores (Tremblay and Sinclair 1986). Juvenile Atlantic sea scallops are mainly found on gravel, small rocks, shells, and among branching animals and plants that permit attachment of juveniles (Thouzeau, Robert, and Smith 1991) and feed on suspended particles, primarily phytoplankton and microzooplankton (Shumway, Selvin, and Schick 1987). Adult sea scallops are filter feeders on phytoplankton and other suspended organic particulates from the water (Shumway, Selvin, and Schick 1987). Adult sea scallops prefer temperatures of 5-18°C (Hart and Chute 2004). Predation on juvenile and adult sea scallops decreases with shell height; common predators include Atlantic cod, wolffish, sculpins, winter and yellowtail flounder, as well as boring polychaetes, sea stars, crabs, and lobsters (Hart and Chute 2004). The New England Fishery Management Council manages Atlantic sea scallops in federal waters under the Atlantic Sea Scallop Fishery Management Plan. As of the 2020 Management Track Assessment, the stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2020).

title: 11  Atlantic surfclam
section: 12.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
12.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Warming bottom water temperatures in the Northwest Atlantic have produced a range shift in Atlantic surfclams from southern, inshore waters of the Mid-Atlantic Bight into deeper waters north and offshore (Timbs, Powell, and Mann 2019) (Stromp et al. 2023). The southern range boundary of the species likely began to migrate northward and offshore in the 1970s (Hofmann et al. 2018). A mass mortality event in the inshore waters of Delmarva occurred in 2000, where surfclams were in a “transient event margin”, a physiological range where scope for growth is negative but temperatures remain non-lethal, leading to decreased energetics and mortality (Weinberg et al. 2005) (Woodin et al. 2013). Thermal stress in surfclams occurs at 21°C and mortality above 25°C (Munroe et al. 2013) (Powell, Kuykendall, and Moreno 2017). Model projections through the end of the 21st century show a positive trend in Atlantic surfclam biomass as their range continues to shift, with biomass expanding in deeper waters off of New Jersey, Long Island, and Southern New England and declines in Georges Bank and Delmarva (Spencer et al. 2026). Warming waters may result in declines in maximum size in the southern portion of the species’ range (Powell et al. 2025). This range shift may lead to potential competition between Atlantic surfclams and ocean quahogs (Arctica islandica) and changes to the spatial extent of the fishery (Spencer et al. 2026) (Stromp et al. 2023).

title: 11  Atlantic surfclam
text:
12.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic surfclams are a benthic biomass dominant species on the Northeast U.S. continental shelf from the Gulf of St. Lawrence to Cape Hatteras, North Carolina (Hofmann et al. 2018). The fishery operates in the Mid-Atlantic Bight and Georges Bank and produces over $30 million in exvessel annual revenue (Murray 2016). Spawning occurs in summer to early fall, with earlier spawning occurring in southern habitats (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Temperature is a determinant for gonadal development and spawning (Loosanoff and Davis 1963). Spawning occurs when temperatures exceed 15°C, and may be delayed due to cold temperatures (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Fertilized eggs develop into planktonic larvae approximately 9 hours after fertilization at 21.7°C and 40 hours at 14°C (Ropes 1980). Larvae mature into the pediveliger stage, a transitional “swimming-crawling” larval stage with development of a foot for burrowing, around 18 days after fertilization (Loosanoff and Davis 1963). Larvae are dispersed by currents (generally southward along the Northeast shelf) and settlement coincides with a relaxation of upwelling events (Fay, Neves, and Pardue 1983). Variable reproductive success has been linked to variability in current dynamics in the Mid-Atlantic Bight (Zhang et al. 2016). Metamorphosis to juveniles, which consists of complete absorption of the velum and settlement to the substrate, occurs anywhere from 19 to 35 days after fertilization depending on temperature (Fay, Neves, and Pardue 1983). Juveniles settle in sandy habitats in the intertidal and up to depths of 178m (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Juveniles and adults are planktivorous siphon feeders, feeding on phytoplankton ≥ 4µm diameter. Food varies with the season, geographic location, and depth of shell bed (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Atlantic surfclams have many predators, including the naticid snails Euspira heros and Neverita duplicata, the sea star Asterias forbesi, lady crabs (Ovalipes ocellatus), Jonah crabs (Cancer borealis) and horseshoe crabs (Limulus polyphemus). Fish predators include haddock (Melanogrammus aeglefinus) and Atlantic cod (Gadus morhua) (Ropes 1980). Age at maturity ranges anywhere from 3 months to 4 years, however size is likely more important to maturation rates (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Atlantic surfclams have a maximum age of 31 years (Cargnelli et al. 1999). The stock is managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Atlantic surfclam and ocean quahog Fishery Management Plan. As of the 2024 Management Track Assessment, the stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring. However, spawning stock biomass continues to decline and is currently at 95% of the target biomass (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2024).

title: 12  Atlantic wolffish
section: 13.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
13.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Atlantic wolffish is a cold water species and has been termed a “temperature seeker” (Kulka et al. 2011). Early life history stages and fish near the southern stock boundary may be the most vulnerable to surface water temperatures exceeding 9-11°C (Rountree 2002). Juvenile Atlantic wolffish exposed to temperatures of 15°C exhibited reduced growth and higher metabolic rates compared to those at 10°C (Hinchcliffe et al. 2025). In the North Sea, Atlantic wolffish have shifted their distribution and decreased abundance at the southern end of their range and in shallow waters (Bluemel et al. 2022). Bianucci et al. (2016) observed a connection between low dissolved oxygen and contraction of the Atlantic wolffish population on the Scotian Shelf. Model results of future dissolved oxygen conditions also extend to areas with high Atlantic wolffish biomass (Bianucci et al. 2016). Similar results were also observed in (Brennan, Blanchard, and Fennel 2016) which predicted declines in dissolved oxygen concentrations and increases in temperature outside of known adult wolffish tolerances.

title: 12  Atlantic wolffish
text:
13.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic wolffish inhabit deep, rocky waters of the Northwest Atlantic from Labrador to the Great South Channel and Georges Bank (Rountree 2002). In the Northwest Atlantic, wolffish are believed to spawn primarily in autumn (Rountree 2002). Spawning is likely temperature dependent (Atlantic Wolffish Biological Review Team 2009). Wolffish will migrate to colder temperatures before the spawning season, and egg laying and guarding behavior could be disrupted if bottom temperatures increase above 10°C (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). However, laboratory experiments suggest that timing may be highly plastic and dependent on local conditions, including photoperiod (McBride et al. 2022). Spatial and temporal variation in spawning may be contributing to the stock’s regional reproductive isolation (Atlantic Wolffish Biological Review Team 2009). Eggs are deposited in rocky dens and incubation time is highly dependent on water temperature, varying between 3 - 9 months (Atlantic Wolffish Biological Review Team 2009). After hatching, prolarvae stay close to the bottom. Larvae consume a broad range of amphipod, crustacean, and fish larvae (Rountree 2002). The prolarval stage can last from a few hours to 6 days (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). The larvae become pelagic shortly after and stay within the water column from 20 days to 2 months, with colder temperatures prolonging the larval period, before migrating back to the bottom (Rountree 2002). Juvenile and adult wolffish occupy the bottom habitats of the continental shelf and slope, using large stones and rocks for shelter and nesting and soft substrates for feeding (Atlantic Wolffish Biological Review Team 2009) (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). Juvenile wolffish primarily consume echinoderms (Atlantic Wolffish Biological Review Team 2009). Adults prey upon hard-shelled invertebrates such as mollusks, crustaceans and echinoderms. Stomach contents have included sea urchins, whelks, cockles, sea clams, brittle stars, crabs, scallops and other shellfish in addition to an occasional redfish (Atlantic Wolffish Biological Review Team 2009) (Rountree 2002). While previous studies have suggested that wolffish migrate inshore/offshore seasonally for spawning or maturity, it is unclear if this is a true migration or if behavioral changes are affecting catchability as eggs are found both inshore and offshore. More recent tagging studies have illustrated that movements in the Northwest Atlantic are limited, but the species has been shown to migrate in the Northeast Atlantic (Rountree 2002) (Simpson et al. 2015) (Gunnarsson et al. 2019). Spiny dogfish, sea raven and Atlantic cod are the dominant predators for juveniles. For adults, the occurrence of predation is low and is not likely to be having a significant effect at the population level (Atlantic Wolffish Biological Review Team 2009). The maximum reported age for wolffish in the Gulf of Maine is 33 years (Fairchild et al. 2015). Most individuals reach maturity by age 6, but this may vary regionally due to temperature (Rountree 2002) (McBride et al. 2022). Atlantic wolffish are managed by the New England Fishery Management Council under the Northeast Multispecies (Groundfish) Fishery Management Plan. As of the 2022 Management Track Assessment, the stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2022).

title: 13  Black sea bass
section: 14.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
14.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Black sea bass in the Mid-Atlantic Bight are currently expanding their distribution northward into the Gulf of Maine (McMahan, Sherwood, and Grabowski 2020). Black sea bass in their newly-expanded range have a less diverse diet, lower condition, and a younger age-at-maturity than their counterparts at the historic northern range of the stock (McMahan, Sherwood, and Grabowski 2020). A laboratory study on Mid-Atlantic black sea bass observed a decline in bodily processes that require oxygen demand above 24°C, suggesting black sea bass will continue to shift their range poleward with warming conditions (Slesinger et al. 2020). Tabandera et al. (2024) found that winter bottom temperature was positively correlated with recruitment deviations in black sea bass. Black sea bass in the southern Mid-Atlantic Bight also appear to occupy a smaller portion of their habitat in years with an increase in cold shelf water on the shelf slope. Northward distributions of black sea bass are resulting in new species interactions and habitat overlaps. In Narragansett Bay, black sea bass juveniles now share nursery habitats with juvenile tautog and cunner, however dietary overlap was size-dependent and ontogenic diet shifts eventually led to niche separation (Bell et al. 2015) (Taylor et al. 2023).

title: 13  Black sea bass
text:
14.8 Life History Synopsis:
Black sea bass are found along the U.S. Atlantic coast from Cape Cod to the Gulf of America in structured habitats such as reefs, shipwrecks, or oyster beds (Drohan, Manderson, and Packer 2007). In the Mid-Atlantic Bight, spawning occurs from April to October, beginning first in the southern portion of their range and northern latitudes starting later in the year (Kenneth W. Able and Fahay 1998). Early larval stages last approximately 72 hrs at 21°C but can last anywhere from 2-6 days depending on temperature (Berlinsky et al. 2004). Afterwards, larvae enter a 21-24 day planktonic phase where they can disperse up to 50 miles by currents (Watanabe et al. 2021). Eggs and pelagic larvae are vulnerable to predation, especially by gelatinous zooplankton (Arai 1988). Larvae settle in nearshore shelf habitats and then migrate to estuarine nurseries to settle and metamorphose into juveniles (occurs at 13-24mm) (K. W. Able, Fahay, and Shepherd 1995). Larvae are abundant at temperatures between 13-21°C and leave estuaries for deeper water when temperatures dip below 14°C (Kendall 1972). Larvae consume microalgae and zooplankton, including rotifers (Richards 1963). Metamorphosis is likely the biggest factor influencing settlement (Musick and Mercer 1977). Structural complexity appears to be an essential component of juvenile black sea bass habitat offshore as well as inshore nurseries throughout the species range. In offshore areas, recently settled fish occur in accumulations of shell on sand substrata, complex microtopographies on exposed clay, on rocky reefs, and on wrecks (K. W. Able, Fahay, and Shepherd 1995). Primary black sea bass settlement habitats are probably located along the near shore continental shelf in accumulations of the shells of bivalves, including Atlantic surfclams (Spisula solidissima) (K. W. Able, Fahay, and Shepherd 1995). Juvenile growth rates impact winter mortality. Smaller juveniles have smaller lipid reserves and higher metabolic rates and therefore face higher predation or starvation risks in the winter (Geissinger et al. 2021). Juveniles, which are diurnal, visual predators, prey on benthic and epibenthic crustaceans (isopods, amphipods, small crabs, sand shrimp, copepods, mysids) and small fish. As they mature, their diet diversifies to include crabs, krill, isopods, shrimp, and small fish (Kimmel 1973). Adult black sea bass perform seasonal inshore/offshore migrations between coastal spawning grounds and offshore overwintering habitats. Migrations are triggered by declining temperatures (around 10-12°C) and begin in the North moving southward (Tabandera et al. 2024). In the Mid-Atlantic Bight, individuals have home ranges as large as 7km2 (Tabandera et al. 2024). Observations suggest that spawning aggregations consist of a single dominant male and a harem of females. These spawning aggregations may rely on visual cues rather than chemical or environmental cues (Tabandera et al. 2024). Black sea bass are protogynous hermaphrodites, with fish changing sex from female to male as they increase in age and size (McGovern et al. 2002). Age of sexual transition varies with latitude with females maturing and undergoing sexual transition at greater ages in northern latitudes (O’Brien, Burnett, and Mayo 1993). On average, age at maturity in the Mid-Atlantic is 2-3 years but can extend up to 5 years (O’Brien, Burnett, and Mayo 1993). Maximum age for females is 8 years and 12 years for males (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2017). The black sea bass fishery in the Northwest Atlantic is divided into two stock subunits (north and south) and is managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council along with the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. As of the 2025 Management Track Assessment, the Mid-Atlantic stock is not overfished or subject to overfishing (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC) 2025).

title: 14  Blue crab
section: 15.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
15.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
As an estuary dependent species, blue crab habitats have been degraded through urbanization impacts including hypoxia, reduction in seagrass beds, and changes in timing and volume of freshwater inputs. Low oxygen conditions may force crabs into shallower, more spatially restricted areas of Chesapeake Bay, increasing their vulnerability to predation or capture. Prolonged hypoxia may limit food resources (mainly infaunal bivalves) in affected areas even after normoxic conditions resume (Aguilar et al. 2008). Glandon, Kilbourne, and Miller (2019) estimated water temperatures in Chesapeake Bay through 2100 and found that a 50% shorter winter will increase overwinter survival rates in blue crab by 20%. Faster growth, a longer growing season, and lower winter mortality would lead to increased productivity (Glandon, Kilbourne, and Miller 2019). Blue crabs may also adapt phenologically to warming temperatures by shifting the timing of biological events such as spawning (Schneider, Fabrizio, and Lipcius 2024). Blue crabs have been observed in the Gulf of Maine, indicating a possible range extension from climate-driven rising temperatures (Johnson 2015).

title: 14  Blue crab
text:
15.8 Life History Synopsis:
The blue crab’s native range is along the western Atlantic coast from Nova Scotia to Argentina, including the Gulf of America (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). The blue crab fishery is the most valuable fishery in the Chesapeake Bay (Hines 2007). In Chesapeake Bay, adults spawn from May to October, however spawning is likely more limited in cooler waters (University of Maryland Center for Environmental Science (UMCES) 2011). To spawn, adults aggregate in low salinity areas of upper estuaries to mate. Adult females then travel to higher salinity spawning grounds (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). The timing of the migration varies with water temperature and occurs earlier in the year at lower latitudes (Aguilar et al. 2008). Eggs (7-14 days) and larvae (40-70 days) are pelagic (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989) and are carried by currents offshore before using physical transport methods to return to bays and eventually estuaries (Millikin and Williams 1984). Larvae require temperatures of 20-30°C and salinities of 10-30 ppt for proper development (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). Settlement into estuarine habitats is associated with Ekman flow from southward wind events resulting in strong subtidal flow into the estuaries. Once in the estuary, settlement and metamorphosis of the megalopae are stimulated by chemical cues associated with appropriate nursery habitat (Epifanio and Cohen 2016). Larvae are phytoplanktivorous, consuming dinoflagellates and copepod nauplii (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). They are preyed upon by a large number of estuarine and marine animals , including other commercially important species such as the striped bass (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). Once reaching the juvenile stage, blue crabs gradually migrate into shallower, less saline waters in upper estuaries and rivers, where they grow and mature. Males, which prefer low-salinity waters, generally migrate farther upstream than do females, which tend to stay in the lower rivers and estuaries (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). Post-larval and juvenile crabs are considered general scavengers, bottom carnivores, detritivores, and omnivores, consuming a wide range of dead and live fish, crabs, organic debris, shrimp, mollusks (including mussels, clams, oysters, and snails), and aquatic plants (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). Adult blue crab prey is very similar to juveniles; they will consume most available prey and freely switch among preferred prey types (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). Juvenile and adult blue crabs are consumed by mammals, sea turtles, birds, and several fishes (Hill, Fowler, and Van Den Avyle 1989). Blue crabs reach maturity at 12-18 months, with a maximum lifespan around 4 years of age and a maximum size of 9 inches carapace length (CL) (University of Maryland Center for Environmental Science (UMCES) 2011) (Millikin and Williams 1984). Adults prefer temperatures of 9-10°C for feeding, growth, and migration (Hines 2007). In the Northwest Atlantic, blue crabs are managed by the Maryland Department of Natural Resources, Virginia Marine Resources Commission, and Potomac River Fisheries Commission. These entities work together with NOAA to conduct stock assessments for blue crab. The Chesapeake Bay blue crab population is not overfished based on current biological reference points, but abundance and recruitment remain low. A benchmark stock assessment is underway to improve understanding of blue crab population dynamics and its fishery (Chesapeake Bay Stock Assessment Committee (CBSAC) 2025).

title: 15  Bluefin tuna
section: 16.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
16.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Projected surface temperatures in the Gulf of America may alter the spatial and temporal extent of Atlantic bluefin tuna spawning. Model results from (Muhling et al. 2011) indicate decreased larval bluefin tuna occurrence in late spring in the Gulf by 39-61% by 2050 and 93-96% by 2100, with early spring spawning becoming more prevalent. Erauskin-Extramiana et al. (2026) also modeled decreased habitat suitability for spawning in the Gulf of America. They predict a poleward shift in Atlantic bluefin tuna distribution and greater spatial overlap with prey in higher latitudes. Nursery habitats in the Gulf of America are impacted by oil spills. Upwards of 5% of predicted spawning habitat within the U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) was impacted by the Deepwater Horizon oil spill (Hazen et al. 2016).

title: 15  Bluefin tuna
text:
16.8 Life History Synopsis:
Western Atlantic bluefin tuna are a highly migratory species observed from Newfoundland to the Gulf of America; they occupy temperate surface waters but can dive to depths of 1000m (Fromentin 2006). Spawning occurs between spring and summer throughout western North Atlantic spawning grounds, namely the Gulf of America (March-June) and Slope Sea (June-September). Spawning in the Slope Sea occurs one to two months later than the Gulf of America due to differences in sea surface temperatures (Baglin 1982). Bluefin tuna eggs hatch after 2 days and larvae remain pelagic for 3-4 weeks (Shropshire et al. 2022). Larval bluefin tuna are found near spawning grounds and dispersed via wind and the margins of ocean currents. Edges of ocean currents and eddies provide access to warmer sea surface temperatures and nutrient-rich waters that support larval foraging of zooplankton prey while avoiding predation (Shropshire et al. 2022). Favorable habitat for bluefin larvae in the Gulf of America was determined to be moderately warm waters (23.5-28.5°C) in the eddies of the Loop Current, and outside of cooler, higher chlorophyll continental shelf waters (Teo, Boustany, and Block 2007). Along the coast of the southeastern United States, larval stages may benefit from nearshore meanders of the Gulf Stream that can produce upwelling of nutrient rich water along the continental shelf edge (Shropshire et al. 2022). Larvae initially feed on zooplankton and progress to a piscivorous diet at a relatively small size (Llopiz and Hobday 2015). Starvation is the largest cumulative source of mortality in early larval stages, and predation on older larvae is a main source of mortality in later stages (McGowan and Richards 1989). Juvenile habitats are located over the continental shelf in the summer and further offshore in the winter. Juvenile life stages follow similar seasonal movements compared to adult life stages, though are generally more restricted to coastal areas (National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) 2006). Juvenile and adult bluefin tuna are opportunistic, generalist predators feeding mainly on zooplanktivorous fishes and crustaceans. Predation of larger fish continues with increasing body size, with juveniles moving from sand lance to mackerel and herring as adults (Logan et al. 2011) (Logan, Golet, and Lutcavage 2015). Adult bluefin tuna migrate seasonally from winter to early summer spawning grounds in the Gulf of America, Straits of Florida, and Slope Sea to summer and fall foraging grounds throughout the Gulf Stream, South and Mid-Atlantic Bight, Gulf of Maine, Nova Scotia Shelf, and east of the Flemish Cap (Block et al. 2001). Age at 50% maturity estimates for Atlantic bluefin tuna vary between 5 to 12 years, with a maximum age of over 30 years (Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS) 2021). NOAA Fisheries, with recommendations from the International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT), manages the Atlantic bluefin tuna fishery in the United States through the Atlantic Highly Migratory Species Management Division. As of 2023, the stock is being managed ICCAT Recommendation 21-10. Population numbers are unknown but the stock is not subject to overfishing (International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) 2022).

title: 16  Bluefish
section: 17.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
17.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
There has been a documented shift in bluefish distribution, spawning, habitat use in the Mediterranean (Sabatés, Martín, and Raya 2012) (Villegas-Hernández, Lloret, and Muñoz 2015). Bluefish distributions in the Northwest Atlantic seem to be shifting north and east (Tyrell et al. 2022). Increasing fall sea surface temperatures may extend spawning season further into the calendar year (Tyrell et al. 2022). If warming alters spawning phenology, there are concerns that juveniles may arrive in estuaries before their prey. Seasonal mismatches have been shown to impact growth, body condition, and reproductive success (Taylor and Able 2006). Changing distributions of prey species as a result of climate change may alter interactions between bluefish and their prey (Whitfield 2026).

title: 16  Bluefish
text:
17.8 Life History Synopsis:
Bluefish inhabit temperate and tropical surface waters in the North Atlantic from Maine to the Atlantic coast of Florida (Shepherd and Packer 2006). Bluefish in the North Atlantic are a single, migratory stock with two discrete spawning periods. The first occurs during March-May near the edge of the continental shelf of the South-Atlantic Bight. The second occurs between June and August in the Mid-Atlantic Bight (Shepherd and Packer 2006). Eggs hatch after 46-48 hours at 18 - 22.2°C to pelagic larvae (Deuel, Clark, and Mansueti 1966). Larvae spawned in the South Atlantic (spring-spawned cohort) are subject to advection north via the Gulf Stream and inshore via warm core rings. Eggs in the southern part of the Mid-Atlantic Bight may be advected south and offshore (Shepherd and Packer 2006). Transport of larvae and/or juveniles across the shelf- slope region may be aided by wind-driven surface flow (Hare and Cowen 1996). Sherman et al. (1984) note that wide distribution of bluefish larvae over the shelf reduces their vulnerability to the predator field and environmental perturbation. Larvae prey on copepods (Shepherd and Packer 2006). Peak abundance of larval bluefish in Southern New England and the Mid-Atlantic Bight coincided with zooplankton production (Sherman et al. 1984). Changes in prey availability and distribution can impact bluefish condition and reproductive success (Taylor and Able 2006). Juvenile bluefish inhabit estuaries, bays, and coastal oceans with sand, oyster beds, and submerged aquatic vegetation habitats (McBride and Conover 1991). Juveniles enter estuaries when temperatures are greater than 13-15°C and leave when temperatures fall below 10-12°C (Smith, Berrien, and Potthoff 1994). Juvenile migration into estuarine habitats appears tied to a shift in eating other fish. In estuaries, adults and juveniles eat whatever taxa are locally abundant (fish, crustaceans, shrimp, and polychaetes). In ocean habitats, young-of-the-year bluefish switch to piscivory predation with increasing size, similar to estuarine habitats (Marks et al. 1996). Bluefish are opportunistic foragers, and the composition of adult bluefish diet depends largely on the available prey, but they consume similar prey items as juvenile bluefish (Shepherd and Packer 2006). Bluefish have long been recognized as a notable predator of forage fish (Tyrell et al. 2022). Adult bluefish conduct seasonal migrations following warmer waters, overwintering in the south and moving north in the spring and summer (Shepherd and Packer 2006). Migrations may be cued by photoperiod as well, and have been observed to shift northward in warm years (Nye et al. 2009). Shortfin mako sharks are identified as a major predator of bluefish and may consume more individuals than removed by the fishery (Wood et al. 2009). Most bluefish are mature by age 2, with approximately half maturing by age 1 (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2015). The maximum age for bluefish in the North Atlantic is 12 years (Salerno, Burnett, and Ibara 2001). In the Northwest Atlantic, bluefish are managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council and Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. The last stock assessment occurred in 2025, where bluefish were found to not be overfished nor is overfishing occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025).

title: 17  Blueline tilefish
section: 18.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
18.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Little research has been conducted on the response of blueline tilefish to climate change. Rising water temperatures likely drove a rapid increase in landings in the Mid-Atlantic throughout the early 21st century as blueline tilefish distributions expanded north beyond their traditional South Atlantic boundary (Pinsky et al. 2018) (Rogers et al. 2019). Blueline tilefish may exhibit a moderately-high risk to achieving optimal biomass and yield due to climate change on productivity (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2025).

title: 17  Blueline tilefish
text:
18.8 Life History Synopsis:
Blueline tilefish have only recently been found on the Northeast U.S. Continental Shelf. They live in crevices or on ledges on the shelf, shelf break, and shelf slope from Georges Bank to Mexico and the Gulf of America (Schmidtke 2017). Blueline tilefish are multiple batch spawners and presumably spawn on/near the bottom (Low 2023). Spawning occurs from March through October in the southern portion range, and May - November in the Mid-Atlantic Bight (Harris, Wyanski, and Powers Mikell 2004) (Schmidtke 2017). Eggs and larvae are estimated to remain pelagic for 4-6 weeks and depend on currents for transport. Eddies and gyres may provide local retention while the Gulf Stream transports eggs and larvae up the East Coast. It seems likely that substantial numbers of blueline larvae are transported out of the eastern Gulf of Mexico, to the South Atlantic, and from the South Atlantic into the Mid-Atlantic over the course of a few weeks (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2025). Juveniles settle to the bottom and occupy benthic habitats around offshore submarine canyons on the shelf edge, constructing burrows in seafloor sediments or within rocks and boulders (Schmidtke 2017). Juveniles and adults consume a diverse array of benthic invertebrates, including polychaetes, crustaceans, and decapods. Gastropods and copepods have also been observed and are likely consumed when diel vertical migrations bring them close to the seafloor (Bielsa and Labisky 1987) (Ross 1982). Age at maturity is up to 3.7 years in the Mid-Atlantic and 5 years in the South Atlantic (Harris, Wyanski, and Powers Mikell 2004) (Low 2023). Maximum age is 37 years, although it is theorized that individuals could possibly reach upwards of 50 years in age (Schmidtke 2017) (Spanik and Ballenger 2023). In the Northwest Atlantic, blueline tilefish are managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Tilefish Fishery Management Plan. Council management of blueline tilefish in the Mid-Atlantic began as a response to increased commercial and recreational landings in the region. The Mid-Atlantic stock has not been assessed. Population levels and fishing status are currently unknown but management measures have been in place since 2017 (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2017).

title: 18  Butterfish
section: 19.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
19.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Butterfish distributions and abundance in the Mid-Atlantic Bight are strongly related to bottom temperature (Kohut et al. 2013). Butterfish have shifted their distribution northward in recent decades. Collie, Wood, and Jeffries (2008) documented an increase in butterfish abundance in Narragansett Bay in the latter half of the 20th century, and they recently have expanded their range into the Gulf of Maine (Suca et al. 2021) (Adams 2022). Their depth distribution has also shifted to inhabiting deeper waters (Suca et al. 2021). An increase in butterfish prevalence in the Gulf of Maine has altered seabird diets, and is replacing preferred prey in common terns (Smith and Craig 2023).

title: 18  Butterfish
text:
19.8 Life History Synopsis:
Butterfish distribution ranges from Newfoundland in Canada to Atlantic Florida, but they are primarily found from the Gulf of Maine to Cape Hatteras. They are a schooling fish and shift distribution in response to changing water temperatures: moving north and inshore over the summer to spawn and south and offshore in the winter to seek warmer water (Cross et al. 1999). Butterfish are broadcast spawners and spawn primarily in the evening or at night, however no direct observations have been made (Horn 1970). In general, butterfish spawn starting in May through early fall, peaking in June-July, although timing is dependent on location (Cross et al. 1999). Egg duration and larval growth is temperature dependent (Colton and Honey 1963), and most eggs and larvae prefer temperatures 9-19°C (Cross et al. 1999). Butterfish eggs and larvae are widely distributed throughout their range from spring to fall as spawning occurs over their entire range. Larvae are associated with the Gulf Stream, although it is not clear how far they travel (Morse, Fahay, and Smith 1987). Later life stages (juveniles and adults) can inhabit surface waters to depth on the continental shelf and into coastal bays and estuaries (Schreiber 1973). Survival is reduced in juveniles and adults below 10°C (Cross et al. 1999). Juvenile and adult butterfish feed mainly on planktonic prey including thaliaceans (primarily Larvacea and Hemimyaria), mollusks (primarily squids), crustaceans, coelenterates, polychaetes, small fishes, and ctenophores (Horn 1970). Recent diet analysis on 2013-2015 trawl specimens illustrates that spring diets are dominated by Calanus copepods, amphipods, larvaceans, and fall diets are dominated by amphipods (Suca et al. 2018). There is little information about larval predation; juvenile and adult butterfish are preyed upon by haddock, silver hake, goosefish, weakfish, bluefish, swordfish, sharks (hammerhead), and longfin inshore squid (Bigelow and Schroeder 1953) (Horn 1970). Butterfish age at maturity is 1-2 years; the maximum lifespan is 6 years although most butterfish live roughly 2-3 years (Horn 1970) (Draganik and Zukowski 1966). In the Northwest Atlantic, butterfish are managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Mackerel, Squid, and Butterfish Fishery Management Plan. As of the 2024 stock assessment, the stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2024).

title: 19  Chub mackerel
section: 20.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
20.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Fluctuations in Atlantic chub mackerel abundance have been reported in the Mid-Atlantic and New England, and may result from environmental influences such as temperature and upwelling. Oceanographic processes likely influence recruitment and distribution, as all life stages of chub mackerel are fully pelagic (Hern’andez and Ortega 2000). In the eastern Atlantic, chub mackerel distributions have shifted northward, with a delayed and prolonged spawning season with increasing latitude (Dom’inguez-Petit et al. 2022). In the Alboran Sea, chub mackerel abundance is vulnerable to variations in sea surface temperature, salinity, currents, and wind patterns (Ayman et al. 2024).

title: 19  Chub mackerel
text:
20.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic chub mackerel is a schooling pelagic species ranging from Nova Scotia to Argentina, including the Gulf of America, and are found on the continental shelf at depths of 250-300m (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2025). They are batch spawners, spawning January through April from North Carolina through the Gulf of America (Daley 2018). Spawning generally occurs in waters greater than 10°C and is possibly triggered by water temperatures between 15- 20°C (Hern’andez and Ortega 2000). Chub mackerel eggs develop for approximately 53-56 hrs at 18-20°C. Larval stages last between 16-24 days, developing faster at higher temperatures (Hern’andez and Ortega 2000). Atlantic chub mackerel grow rapidly during the first year of life, reaching maturity around 2 years of age (Daley 2018) and can live up to 13 years (Hern’andez and Ortega 2000). Chub mackerel are generalist predators of zooplankton and other pelagic prey including mysids, euphausids, decapod larvae, copepods, amphipods, small fish, cephalopods. Cannibalism has been observed in this species (Hern’andez and Ortega 2000). There is little data on chub mackerel predation in the Northwest Atlantic. The Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council funded a study on diet composition of marlins and tunas and found very few chub mackerel in the stomachs of yellowfin tuna and white marlin (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2025). In 2019, the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council approved Amendment 21 to the Mackerel, Squid, and Butterfish Fishery Management Plan. This Amendment established conservation and management measures for Atlantic chub mackerel in the Northwest Atlantic (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2019). There is currently no stock assessment of chub mackerel in the Mid-Atlantic. Biomass is assumed to be at a sustainable level based on stock productivity and low fishery capacity (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2025).

title: 20  Cobia
section: 21.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
21.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Cobia off the eastern U.S. Atlantic coast currently overwinter offshore in waters south of North Carolina. Suitable habitat increases in the summer months with warming temperatures. Habitat distribution models suggest increased habitat suitability northward, shifting distributions poleward, and possible earlier seasonal migrations (Crear et al. 2020). This shift northward could prompt revision of management regulations as the fishery expands to new states or regions. Early life stage cobia may be somewhat resistant to projected rises in CO2 levels (Ebanks 2013), although juvenile cobia in a laboratory study exhibited poor growth performance and reduced feeding at elevated temperatures (Le et al. 2024).

title: 20  Cobia
text:
21.8 Life History Synopsis:
Cobia are a pelagic, continental shelf fish distributed from Massachusetts to Argentina but are most abundant from Virginia to the Gulf of America (Shaffer and Nakamura 1989). Two stocks are recognized in the United States: one along the Atlantic coast south to Georgia and the other along the Atlantic coast of Florida into the Gulf (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2020). Cobia are batch spawners, and move inshore to spawn when temperatures reach 20-25°C. They spawn over a period of 4-6 weeks, peaking in June-July in Virginia (Joseph, Norcross, and Massmann 1964). Cobia eggs hatch within 36 hours of fertilization. Given the short time frame between fertilization and hatching, it is likely that eggs and larvae do not travel far from spawning grounds (Shaffer and Nakamura 1989). Little is known about the feeding habits of larval and juvenile cobia. Adult cobia are generalist carnivores, feeding on benthic-epibenthic crustaceans (Portunids and Callinectes sp.), teleosts, cephalopods, and elasmobranchs (Smith 1995). Juveniles are pelagic and often associated with structure for feeding and shelter. Juveniles and adults can be found near shipwrecks, buoys, shellfish beds, and submerged aquatic vegetation (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2020). Few predators of cobia in U.S. Atlantic waters are known, although dolphinfish (Coryphaena hippurus) have been reported to prey on small cobia (Shaffer and Nakamura 1989). Over 50% of cobia are mature by age 2, and can live up to 15 years (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2020). Adults are highly migratory. Cobia exhibit a general migratory trend of overwintering in south Florida and the Florida Keys waters, and then migrating north along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts in late winter-early spring. They likely return to south Florida coastal waters in the late fall (Hendon, Franks, and Fulford 2008). The Atlantic migratory cobia are managed by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. The population is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring as of the last benchmark stock assessment in 2020 (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2020).

title: 21  Eastern oyster
section: 22.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
22.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Climate change, warmer temperatures, and perhaps different tidal ranges, may increase competition for resources with current competitors, like hooked mussel, invasive species such as Didemnum sp., or increase exposure to predators like veined rapa whelk (White and Wilson 1996). Depletion of oyster beds will also likely have negative effects for many marine species and habitats, for they provide refuge and homes for many fish and invertebrates species and help prevent erosion (Eastern Oyster Biological Review Team (EOBRT) 2007). Hurricanes can cover oysters in silt, wash them off beds, or impose extended “freshettes” on oyster beds to the degree that they are significantly impaired and need restoration. This may become more problematic, for hurricanes are thought to increase with increased warming temperatures and climate change (Perret et al. 1999). Since adult oysters are sessile and inhabit mainly highly vulnerable habitats in areas with high climate exposure, their spawning habitat is also highly vulnerable (Farr et al. 2021).

title: 21  Eastern oyster
text:
22.8 Life History Synopsis:
Eastern oysters are found along the eastern U.S. from the Gulf of St. Lawrence to the Gulf of America. They inhabit brackish and salty waters, forming beds or reefs by attaching to each other or other hard substrate (Ozbay et al. 2014). Eastern oysters are a keystone species, and provide valuable habitat for benthic and intertidal organisms. Oysters are sessile filter feeders, contributing to increased water quality through reduced sediment suspension and nutrient uptake and sequestration (Ozbay et al. 2014). Eastern oyster populations in the Mid-Atlantic and Gulf declined in the 19th and 20th centuries, mainly from anthropogenic factors such as land use, habitat degradation, and overharvesting. Disease mortality from Dermo and MSX continues to diminish oyster recovery (Eastern Oyster Biological Review Team (EOBRT) 2007). Aquaculture production of oysters contributes to population recovery and habitat restoration programs across the East Coast (MacKenzie 1981). Oysters spawn during the summer in the North and Mid-Atlantic and throughout most of the year in the South Atlantic when temperatures remain above 20°C and salinities above 10ppt (Berringan et al. 1991). The first larval stage (trochophore) is formed four to six hours following fertilization. Larvae normally settle two to three weeks after hatching, but can last up to 2 months (Eastern Oyster Biological Review Team (EOBRT) 2007). Larvae move both by passive and active (as development progresses) transport, although larval transport in estuaries seems to be minimal. A balance is reached where the larvae can find suitable areas to settle while still being retained in estuaries (Eastern Oyster Biological Review Team (EOBRT) 2007). Settlement ends at the end of the larval stage, and the larval duration is highly temperature dependent (Shumway 1996). Fritz et al. (1984) found that planktotrophic oyster larvae fed upon a variety of phytoplankton taxa (coccoids, centrate diatoms, pennate diatoms, dinoflagellates, and flagellates). Baldwin and Newell (1991) found that the larvae are actually omnivorous, preying on both autotrophic and heterotrophic plankton. Predation on oyster larvae is extremely high by filter-feeders in the water column and in the benthos. Along the Atlantic coast, drills have been responsible for mortality on 33% of spat in Long Island Sound. Oysters can compete with other mussels and barnacles for shell and substrate spaces, resulting in increased mortality when competitors are abundant (Kennedy 1996). Juveniles and adults feed primarily on phytoplankton and suspended detritus, relying on food-laden water being moved past them in order to extract food from the water column (Langdon and Newell 1996). Maturity occurs between 1 and 3 years of age (Florida State University Marine Lab n.d.). Several ages have been proposed, the most common being 20 years. Oysters in Maryland appear to survive at at least 10 years based on plantings at restoration sites which are not thought to have received natural spat sets. It is likely that the maximum longevity is substantially longer than 10 years (Maryland Department of Natural Resources 2025). Wild and aquaculture harvest is managed by individual jurisdictions or states, and management guidelines vary (Ozbay et al. 2014).
