title: 22  Golden tilefish
section: 23.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
23.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Golden tilefish prefer a very narrow temperature range (8-17°C); increased ocean acidification may have negative impacts on survival, growth, and abundance (Salois et al. 2024). Nesslage et al. (2021) identified a correlation between negative Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation (AMO) and positive North Atlantic Oscillation (NAO) and golden tilefish recruitment class pulses. Catch per unit effort is influenced by temperature and oceanographic currents (Gulf Stream and Labrador Current) (Nesslage et al. 2021). A large golden tilefish die-off and resulting fishery collapse in the spring of 1882 has been correlated with meanders in the Gulf Stream, suggesting that they are very sensitive to temperature changes (Fisher et al. 2014). Changes to weather patterns and a rise in stormy conditions along with very cold bottom temperatures can drive golden tilefish off the shelf and cause a decrease in fishing (Salois et al. 2024).

title: 22  Golden tilefish
text:
23.8 Life History Synopsis:
Golden tilefish inhabit continental shelf and slope waters along the entire U.S. East and Gulf Coasts, although they are most abundant from Nantucket to Cape May, New Jersey (Steimle 1999). Two genetically-different tilefish stocks have been identified in United States waters: the Mid-Atlantic Bight and south of Cape Hatteras into the Gulf of America (Steimle 1999). Individuals of many species off Cape Hatteras were smaller and less active than their conspecifics off Virginia. Tilefish are classified as serial or fractional spawners from March to November with a peak in activity in June (Idelberger 1988). However, with serial or fractional spawners there is some doubt whether all of the eggs in the ovaries are released during a single seasonal spawning cycle. Residual eggs could be resorbed during the winter (Grimes et al. 1988). Artificially-fertilized eggs hatched in 40 hours at 22-24.6°C; it is likely that egg duration increases in colder temperatures (Fahay and Berrien 1981). The duration of the larval stage is unknown. Movements of water masses along the shelf break may play a role in recruitment success, including larval survival and retention near spawning grounds. Strong recruitment pulses have been linked to shifts in the Gulf Stream Index, AMO, and NAO (Salois et al. 2024). Little is known about larval diets, but they likely prey on zooplankton, particularly Calanus (Steimle 1999). Common predators of larval and juvenile tilefish are monkfish, spiny dogfish, and bottom-dwelling sharks (Steimle 1999). Post-larval tilefish are primarily sedentary and non-migratory. They settle in waters approximately 8-17°C, known as the “warm belt” (where shelf and slope water meet) on the outer continental shelf and upper slope of the Northwest Atlantic (Able et al. 1982). Juveniles and adults often occupy simple vertical shaft burrows in semi-lithified clay of the shelf break, submarine canyon walls, and flanks around 100-200m depth (Able et al. 1982). Lobster pots, red crab traps, ship wrecks and other artificial structures have also been used by tilefish (Steimle 1999). Diet shifts with size, with crustaceans, mollusks, and echinoderms dominating young/small tilefish diets. Adult tilefish are opportunistic benthic foragers, feeding on shrimp, crabs, snails, worms, anemones, sea cucumbers, smaller fish, squid, and bivalves. They are known to scavenge on fresh material and reject rancid bait. Adults will also consume anthropogenic material (Steimle 1999). Age at maturity is typically 2-4 years (Steimle 1999), with a maximum age up to 45 years (Bigelow and Schroeder 1953). The Northwest Atlantic stock is managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Tilefish Fishery Management Plan. The most recent benchmark stock assessment occurred in 2024. The stock is not overfished, but overfishing is occurring and the Mid-Atlantic stock is reduced to end overfishing (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2024).

title: 23  Haddock
section: 24.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
24.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
In the Northeast Atlantic, early life stage haddock and cod compete for food and later life stage cod prey upon haddock. Changing climate and the increasing number of warm years increases cod abundance, causing a decline in haddock (Durant et al. 2020). Haddock abundance on Georges Bank has increased significantly in recent years and may coincide with a rise in extreme ocean temperatures or phytoplankton blooms (Friedland et al. 2008) (Wang, Gao, and McCurdy 2024). A large recruitment event in Georges Bank haddock in 2003 was hypothesized to be related to the timing of the fall bloom and enhanced adult condition, affecting reproductive output (Friedland et al. 2008). Spatial distribution changes of haddock were modeled using bottom trawl survey data, and results showed range expansion and contraction in both spring and fall. Higher temperatures correspond with northward distribution shifts on Georges Bank (Wang, Gao, and McCurdy 2024). Future warming may shift haddock’s southern range boundary, making it difficult to define stock boundaries.

title: 23  Haddock
text:
24.8 Life History Synopsis:
In the Northwest Atlantic, haddock are found from Newfoundland to Cape May, New Jersey but are most abundant in the Gulf of Maine and on Georges Bank (Brodziak 2005). The stock is managed in two subunits for the Gulf of Maine and Georges Bank (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2024a) (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2024b). Haddock primarily spawn on the Northeast peak of Georges Bank and in the Great South Channel. Spawning occurs from January to July with a delay in peak spawning time with increasing latitude (Smith and Morse 1985). Spawning distribution is influenced more by restrictive spawning area and bottom type conditions than by temperature variation (Page and Frank 1989). At water temperatures typical of Georges Bank, haddock eggs hatch in about 15 days. Larvae metamorphose into juveniles in roughly 30-42 days at lengths of 2-3 cm (Page and Frank 1989). Small juveniles initially live and feed in the epipelagic zone and remain in the upper part of the water column for 3-5 months (Fahay 1983). The retention of haddock larvae in suitable nursery areas is an important factor in determining year class strength of Georges Bank haddock. The clockwise gyre around the main portion of Georges Bank provides a physical mechanism to retain haddock larvae on the Bank. There is limited information on retention of larval haddock in the Gulf of Maine. Larvae can potentially be advected as far south as the Mid-Atlantic Bight, but local retention or transport to nursery habitats to the southwest of Georges Bank are important to strong recruitment (Brodziak 2005). Haddock larvae are passive foragers on less motile prey: invertebrate eggs, copepods, and phytoplankton (Marak 1960). Planktonic prey declines after juveniles become demersal, feeding on ophiuroids and polychaetes instead of copepods and pteropods (Brodziak 2005). Common predators of juvenile haddock include spiny dogfish, skates, and other groundfish. Gray seals also prey on adult haddock (Brodziak 2005). Adult haddock are indiscriminate consumers of benthic, sedentary or slow-moving invertebrates: crustaceans, annelids, polychaetes, mollusks, and echinoderms. Prey consumption varies by area and prey availability indicating adaptability (Tam et al. 2016). Juvenile and adult haddock are demersal and usually found between 40-150m depth. Preferred bottom types include gravel, pebbles, clay and smooth hard sand, particularly smooth areas between rocky patches (Klein-MacPhee 2002). Age at maturity ranges from 1-4 years with a maximum age of 22 years (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2014). The Georges Bank and Gulf of Maine haddock stocks are managed by the New England Fishery Management Council under the Northeast Multispecies (Groundfish) Fishery Management Plan. Both stocks are not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2024a) (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2024b).

title: 24  Longfin squid
section: 25.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
25.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Cephalopods have been shown to be sensitive to environmental variability and climate change, due to their short lifespan (Moustahfid et al. 2021). Inshore squids, such as longfin, may have complex responses to climate change. As temperatures increase, squids will likely hatch smaller and earlier, grow faster, mature at a smaller size, and have shorter life spans. They may also require more food and oxygen, but a reduced capacity to cope without food (Pecl and Jackson 2008). Longfin squid recruitment may negatively be affected by impacts of increasing ocean acidification on the paralarval stage (Arkhipkin et al. 2015). Longfin squid raised under elevated pCO2 increased hatching time (~24 hours longer) and had shorter mantle lengths as well as significantly reduced statolith surface area (by 25%) and abnormally shaped and porous statoliths (Kaplan et al. 2013). Abundance and distribution changes have been linked to the North Atlantic Oscillation, Gulf Stream, and surface fronts on the outer continental shelf (Dawe et al. 2007) (Hendrickson, Gangopadhyay, and Moustahfid 2023) (Manderson et al. 2011).

title: 24  Longfin squid
text:
25.8 Life History Synopsis:
In the Northwest Atlantic, longfin squid are most abundant from Georges Bank to Cape Hatteras although their range expands from Newfoundland to the Gulf of Venezuela (Jacobson 2005). The North Atlantic stock undertakes seasonal migrations: moving offshore in the fall to overwinter along the shelf edge and slope and migrating inshore in the spring following warming water temperatures to spawn (Hatfield and Cadrin 2002). More recent studies after the development of statolith aging techniques highlight a peak in spawning from late spring to summer (Hatfield and Cadrin 2002) (Macy and Brodziak 2001) and hatching in late summer (Jacobson 2005). Some spawning occurs throughout the year both inshore and offshore depending on hatching month and length-at-age (Macy III 1994) (Macy and Brodziak 2001), with winter spawning thought to occur primarily south of Cape Hatteras (Hatfield and Cadrin 2002). Eggs incubate for 11 - 27 days on sandy or muddy bottoms attached to solid substrate, with faster development times are warmer temperatures. The duration of the larval stage is unclear, but they occupy coastal surface waters. Once juveniles, they transition from inhabiting surface waters to a demersal lifestyle (Jacobson 2005). The Gulf Stream and warm core rings may facilitate the recruitment transport of larval and juvenile squid northward, but potential for inputs to the population from the Southeast U.S. Continental Shelf and offshore are difficult to quantify (Hatfield and Cadrin 2002). Juveniles and adults are generally demersal and inhabit the continental shelf and upper slope to depths of 400 m. This varies seasonally, with adults occupying shallower habitats in summer and fall, and deeper habitats in the winter. Diel vertical migrations have been reported (Jacobson 2005). Juveniles consume planktonic organisms, primarily copepods. Their preferred prey type varies with size (Jacobson 2005). Larger individuals feed on crustaceans and small fish. Diets shift seasonally from crustacean-dominated in the spring to fish-dominated in the fall. Cannibalism is observed in individuals larger than 5 cm (Jacobson 2005). Longfin squid are prey for a variety of finfish, marine mammals, and birds (Jacobson 2005). Most statolith-aging studies suggest that longfin squid have a short lifespan of around 1 year or less, reaching maturity shortly before spawning and senescence (Macy and Brodziak 2001). Longfin squid are managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Mackerel, Squid, and Butterfish Fishery Management Plan. The stock is not overfished, and overfishing reference points are not available (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2025).

title: 25  Monkfish
section: 26.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
26.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
In Icelandic waters, monkfish distribution changes and increased stock size have been correlated with rising water temperatures and expanding suitable habitat (Solmundsson, Jonsson, and Bjornsson 2007). Monkfish movement and relative abundance on Georges Bank is driven by seasonal changes in bottom temperature. Their distribution may shift in response to future changes in thermal habitat, highlighting the need for further management strategies (Siemann et al. 2018). Maximum size of monkfish in the Northwest Atlantic has decreased, and is related to seasonality and bottom temperature (Charbonneau et al. 2020).

title: 25  Monkfish
text:
26.8 Life History Synopsis:
Monkfish are a bottom dwelling fish ranging from the northern Gulf of St. Lawrence to Cape Hatteras. The commercial fishery operates from Maine to North Carolina (Steimle, Morse, and Johnson 1999). Spawning takes place from spring through early autumn, progressing from south to north over a 6 month period (Richards, Nitschke, and Sosebee 2008). Pelagic larvae hatch from egg veils (up 12 m long and 1.5 m wide) 1-3 weeks after being laid, depending on temperature. Larvae remain in the egg veil 2-3 days after hatching. Monkfish larvae feed on zooplankton, including copepods, crustacean larvae, and chaetognaths (Bigelow and Schroeder 1953). Larval to juvenile transition occurs at 5-10 cm TL (total length) when the elongate fins and body gradually assume the adult form (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2010). Once larvae transition to juveniles, they move to the benthos. Juveniles and adults settle on mud, gravelly sand, algae, and rock substrates (Steimle, Morse, and Johnson 1999). Once moved to the bottom, juveniles can eat sand lance, but invertebrates such as red shrimp and squid make up the majority of their diet. The amount of invertebrates eaten by juveniles decreases as they get older (Steimle, Morse, and Johnson 1999). Monkfish are ambush predators and opportunistic feeders; prey found in their stomachs include a variety of benthic and pelagic species. Monkfish are also cannibalistic and can eat sea birds and diving ducks. Adults spend most of their time resting on the bottom, but have been reported at the surface after a storm event, preying on seabirds (Richards, Nitschke, and Sosebee 2008). Large monkfish have few predators, but smaller monkfish are common prey for swordfish, sharks, and skates (Steimle, Morse, and Johnson 1999). Monkfish move by slow swimming or by using their sturdy pectoral fins to “walk.” Despite slow speeds, juveniles and adults migrate in response to thermal conditions, spawning, or food availability (Steimle, Morse, and Johnson 1999). Monkfish were found on Georges Bank in summer and early fall, and found in deeper waters/on the edge of the bank in winter months, suggesting that movements may be influenced by temperature (Rountree, Gr"oger, and Martins 2006). Age at maturity is 4-5 years, longer for females and for the Mid-Atlantic stock (Richards, Nitschke, and Sosebee 2008). Maximum age is listed at 7 years for males and 12-14 years for females (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2010). Monkfish in the Northwest Atlantic are divided into two stocks: the Gulf of Maine/Northern Georges Bank and Southern Georges Bank/Mid-Atlantic. Both stocks do not have stock assessments that provide indices on overfishing and overfished status, although an index based assessment is used for catch advice (Deroba 2022).

title: 26  Northern Quahog
section: 27.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
27.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Temperature is the decisive factor for final gamete maturation in northern quahogs. Kennish and Olsson (1975) cited 21 to 25°C as the required or preferred temperature range for spawning. Metamorphosis is inhibited at salinities below 17.5 to 20 ppt (Stanley and DeWitt 1983). Increased freshwater intrusions (man-made or otherwise) have resulted in clam die offs. Eutrophication of habitat has resulted in reduced growth and health of stocks (Carmichael, Shriver, and Valiela 2004). Recent research suggests that quahogs are relatively resilient to suboxic conditions, which may help protect them from predators that are more sensitive to these conditions (Altieri and Witman 2006). Northern quahogs exposed to higher temperatures and elevated pCO2 led to decreased shell hardness and energy deficiency (Ivanina et al. 2013). Alternatively, northern quahogs exposed to marine heat wave conditions exhibited increased absorption efficiency, respiration rate, excretion rate, and scope for growth, indicating their ability to recover from persistent or repeated marine heat waves (Liang et al. 2024).

title: 26  Northern Quahog
text:
27.8 Life History Synopsis:
The northern quahog, or hard clam, is a hard shell clam inhabiting intertidal and subtidal waters from the Gulf of St. Lawrence south to Florida (Kraeuter and Castagna 2001). Spawning occurs from May through August, depending on latitude and temperature. Female hard clams require 2 - 2.5 months to spawn out completely, but the greatest release of eggs is during the initial spawning of the season (Stanley and DeWitt 1983). Eggs hatch within 12-14 hours. Larvae are planktonic until metamorphosis, which occurs within 5-7 days at 30°C, or 16-24 days at 18°C (Stanley and DeWitt 1983) (Rice and Pechenik 1992). Eggs may be carried by currents up to 25km from spawning sites. Larvae feed on phytoplankton, primarily dinoflagellates and use physical transport mechanisms to travel to the entrance of estuaries (Stanley and DeWitt 1983). The clams prefer to settle on sandy substrate with a few small rocks and/or shells, and set more densely in sand than mud (MacKenzie 1979). Juveniles likely consume phytoplankton or small zooplankton. The adult hard clam feeds by filtering out plankton and microorganisms that are carried along the bottom by currents (Stanley and DeWitt 1983), burrowing into the sediment and leaving their siphons exposed to feed. However, adults cease filter feeding at 6°C (Kraeuter and Castagna 2001). They are preyed upon by gastropods, crabs, and starfish (Stanley and DeWitt 1983). These clams have slow growth rates, reaching maturity at 1-3 years with a maximum lifespan of 12-20 years (Bricelj 1992), although they have been found up to 46 years (Ridgway et al. 2011). There are numerous reports of isolated incidents of hard clam disease causing high mortality rates. Disease may be related to a parasite (QPX - Quahog parasite unknown). The prevalence of QPX appears to be increasing (Smolowitz, Leavitt, and Perkins 1998) (Dove, Bowser, and Cerrato 2004). Quahogs occur in areas of harmful algal blooms (HABs) and may act to reduce prevalence and length of HABs due to filtration. Negative effects of HABs on quahog have been noted, specifically the presence of HABs have been shown to impact growth rates (Cerrato et al. 2004). Because these clams are primarily estuarine and inhabit specific bays and coastal zones, the fishery is managed on a localized, state-by-state basis rather than by federal fishery councils. Primary management tools include minimum legal size limits (typically 50 mm), gear restrictions, and daily bag limits (North Carolina Division of Marine Fisheries (NCDMF) 2025) (New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection (NJDEP) 2014).

title: 27  Ocean pout
section: 28.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
28.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Nye et al. (2009) identified an increase in depth of occurrence of ocean pout in the Gulf of Maine. Although ocean pout have been reported to be quite hardy, they appear to have limited tolerance of hypoxic and anoxic conditions; they are common victims of these conditions which occasionally occur in the New York Bight (Azarovitz et al. 1976). Ocean pout are sluggish swimmers, which can explain their inability to avoid stressful conditions, such as hypoxia (Steimle et al. 1999). Killen, Brown, and Gamperl (2008) found that young ocean pout from Newfoundland do not show thermal compensation in response to long-term temperature changes.

title: 27  Ocean pout
text:
28.8 Life History Synopsis:
Ocean pout are a non-migratory, bottom dwelling fish found along the Atlantic coast from Labrador to Delaware (Steimle et al. 1999). Spawning occurs in the late summer through early winter (peak in September-October) with earlier peaks (August-October) in the south (Wilk and Morse 1979). Eggs are laid in gelatinous masses in a sheltered place on the bottom, such as rocky crevices, where they are guarded either by one or both parents until hatching (Steimle et al. 1999). Eggs develop in 2-3 months, temperature dependent (Olsen and Merriman 1946). Larvae are about 30 mm long at hatching (Clark and Livingstone 1982) and live at the bottom at temperatures below 10°C (Steimle et al. 1999). Juvenile and adult ocean pout use both open and rough habitats. Sedberry (1983) reported that juveniles were common during the summer on the sandy, mid- to outer-continental shelf (approximately 35-95 m). In the Gulf of Maine, juveniles occur in shallow coastal waters around rocks and attached algae, and in rivers with saline bottom waters (Auster 1985). MacDonald (1983) reported that ocean pout feed by sorting mouthfuls of sediments for infauna and do not use visual cues to stimulate feeding; they maintained that ocean pout were not very effective at capturing moving prey and rarely left the bottom to feed. Auster (1985) and Auster, Malatesta, and LaRosa (1995), however, considered ocean pout an “ambush predator” that waits in sediment depressions for prey to approach or drift by in the current. Juveniles feed primarily on gammarid amphipods and polychaetes (Sedberry 1983). Adult ocean pout feed on a variety of benthic invertebrates, including polychaetes, mollusks, crustaceans, and echinoderms (Steimle et al. 1999). Adults reach maturity at 2+ years (Steimle et al. 1999), with a maximum age of 18 years (Hoenig 1983). Although they do not migrate, ocean pout move seasonally within a region: offshore in late summer and inshore in the spring in the Gulf of Maine, and offshore to cooler waters in the summer returning inshore in the fall in Southern New England and Georges Bank (Steimle et al. 1999). Ocean pout are managed by the New England Fishery Management Council under the Northeast Multispecies Fishery Management Plan. As of the 2022 management track assessment, the stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2022).

title: 28  Ocean quahog
section: 29.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
29.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Radiocarbon aging on ocean quahog shells suggest past (200 years) ocean quahog distributions inshore of their current range, indicating a range shift coinciding with the regression of the Cold Pool after the Little Ice Age (LeClaire et al. 2022). With settlement occurring at the end of the larval stage, it may be presumed that temperature impacts settlement rate. Weinberg, Dahlgren, and Halanych (2002) note that an increase in temperature would shift the species offshore and north. Future warming conditions are predicted to result in range shifts in Atlantic surfclams that would lead to potential competition for food and space between surfclams and ocean quahogs (Stromp, Powell, and Mann 2023) (Spencer et al. 2024).

title: 28  Ocean quahog
text:
29.8 Life History Synopsis:
Ocean quahogs are bivalve mollusks that inhabit the Northeast U.S. Continental Shelf from Newfoundland to Cape Hatteras (Cargnelli et al. 1999). The ocean quahog is among the longest-lived and slowest growing of marine bivalves, reaching maturity around 13 years of age and harvestable size at age 20 (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Maximum ages have been documented as high as 507 years old, with ages over 300 years old more common in the Northwest Atlantic (Sower et al. 2023). Spawning occurs either in summer or fall, and can extend for months, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column at low levels over time (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Multiple annual spawnings may occur at the individual and population levels (Mann 1982). Eggs and larvae drift with currents until metamorphosis, then settle to the bottom (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 1997). Larval development, at minimum, lasts for 55 days and decreases with increasing temperature (Lutz et al. 1981). Larvae go through three stages of development (trochophores, veligers and pediveligers) with the duration of each stage being temperature dependent (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Van de Water Lewis (1997) noted that wind-forced circulation may play a significant role in planktonic and benthic recruitment on Georges Bank and that interannual differences in these winds can yield very different abundance patterns over the bank. Once settled to the bottom, juvenile ocean quahogs are found offshore in sandy substrates, but may survive in muddy intertidal environments if protected from predators (Kraus, Beal, and Chapman 1989). Cross-shelf distributions of juveniles and adults remain within a 10°C optimal temperature range, as temperatures above 16°C limit distribution (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Ocean quahogs are suspension feeders on phytoplankton, using their relatively short siphons which are extended above the surface of the substrate to pump in water (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Juvenile and small ocean quahogs are commonly preyed upon by crustaceans (rock crabs, sea stars) and fish (haddock, cod) (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Ocean quahogs are managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Atlantic Surfclam-Ocean Quahog Fishery Management Plan. The species is mainly harvested off of Maine and Long Island. The last stock assessment was conducted in 2019, where the stock was not overfished and overfishing was not occurring (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2025).

title: 29  Pollock
section: 30.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
30.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Because of the narrow range for when spawning occurs (4.5-6°C) (Cargnelli et al. 1999) (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002), increase in temperatures and climate change could have implications on the stock’s success. The center of biomass for Northwest Atlantic pollock has not shifted and distribution has not moved northward, although they have been recently found at greater depths (Nye et al. 2009). Murawski (1993) noted a range contraction, increased depth of occurrence, and increased temperature of occurrence in pollock and other deep-water fish in the Gulf of Maine.

title: 29  Pollock
text:
30.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic pollock are found in the Northwest Atlantic from the Grand Banks and Gulf of St. Lawrence to the Delmarva Peninsula, with highest abundances on the Scotian Shelf and in the Gulf of Maine (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Spawning takes place from September to April on the Scotian Shelf and November to February in the Gulf of Maine (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Optimal egg development occurs between 2.2 and 8.9°C (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Eggs are pelagic and free-floating, usually found in water 50-250 m in depth. Larvae are normally occurring from the shore out to the 200 m depth contour, but have been reported in waters as deep as 1550 m (Hardy 1978). Eggs and larvae undergo significant movement away from the spawning area as they drift with currents. For example, larvae from the northern Gulf of Maine are thought to originate from spawning grounds in the southern Gulf of Maine (Hardy 1978). Larvae are pelagic, consuming generally the most abundant copepods in their preferred size range, and metamorphose into juveniles at 3-4 months of age (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Juveniles have been reported over a wide variety of substrates, including sand, mud, or rocky bottom and vegetation (Ojeda and Dearborn 1990). Inshore subtidal and intertidal zones are utilized by age 0-1 juveniles and serve as important nursery areas. Age 2+ juveniles move offshore, inhabiting depths of 130-150 m (Hardy 1978). The primary prey of juvenile pollock are crustaceans. Euphausiids, in particular Meganyctiphanes norvegica, are the most important crustacean prey of juveniles (Tyler 1972). Fish and mollusks make up a smaller proportion of the juvenile diet; however, in some cases fish may play a more important role in the diet (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). Pollock reach maturity at 3-7 years of age, longer for females, and reach a maximum age of 24 years (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2013). Adults inhabit a wide range of depths, 35-365 m, but most occur within the 137 m depth contour and depths of 100-125 m are preferred (Scott 1982). They prefer temperatures 0-14°C , although they tend to avoid temperatures > 11°C and < 3°C (Scott 1982). For most of the year, adult pollock undergo minor inshore-offshore movements linked to temperature. Pollock migration rates vary with stock areas, but there is considerable movement across the Scotian Shelf and Gulf of Maine (Mayo, McGlade, and Clark 1989). The diet of adults consists of, in order of decreasing importance, euphausiids, fish and mollusks. M. norvegica is the single most important prey item and Atlantic herring is the most important fish species (Steele 1963). The primary predator of adult pollock are monkfish, although spiny dogfish and other pollock also prey on adults (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Atlantic pollock are managed by the New England Fishery Management Council under the Northeast Multispecies Fishery Management Plan. The stock is not currently overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2024).

title: 30  Red drum
section: 31.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
31.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Red drum recruitment has been correlated with wind directions and sea surface temperature (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024). In North Carolina, the duration and magnitude of N and NE winds in August was twice as strong in high recruitment years. Elevated nearshore sea surface temperatures and coastal productivity (high chlorophyll a) were positively correlated with strong year classes (Goldberg, Paramore, and Scharf 2022). Red drum acclimated to higher temperatures had a higher standard metabolic rate, decreased critical oxygen threshold, and reduced ability to recover from exercise. Crude oil exposure (hypoxic conditions) also resulted in lower metabolic rates and reduced ability to recover from exercise, and combined with climate-induced increases in temperature, are likely to impact red drum respiratory performance and fitness (Ackerly and Esbaugh 2021). Laboratory experiments on early life stage red drum resulted in decreased survival and increased heart rate when exposed to elevated pCO2 ; however the effects were less severe on red drum compared to similar studies on other species (Lonthair, Ern, and Esbaugh 2017).

title: 30  Red drum
text:
31.8 Life History Synopsis:
Red drum, or redfish, are a popular recreational fish species found in estuarine and inshore waters from Massachusetts to Florida. The stock is divided into northern and southern subunits from New Jersey to North Carolina and South Carolina to Florida, respectively (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024). Spawning occurs within 45-60 days between August and October and where currents can move larvae/eggs into nearby estuaries and nursery habitats (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024). Optimal spawning temperatures in laboratory studies ranged between 22 - 25°C, with spawning observed from 22 - 30°C (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024). Eggs hatch within 24-36 hours (Peters and McMichael 1987). Larvae settle at approximately 17 days old in Florida estuaries (Rooker, Holt, and Holt 1998). Temperatures of 25°C and salinities around 30 are optimal for hatching success and larval survival. Larvae growth rates were also correlated with ocean temperatures in the Gulf of America (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024) (Comyns et al. 1989). Larvae in Florida consume abundant copepods, whereas juveniles consume abundance amphipods, shrimp, and mysids, switching to crabs and fish as they grow (Peters and McMichael 1987). Juveniles rely on structural nursery habitats such as seagrasses for protection from predators. Juveniles are most abundant in estuarine waters and inlets, while fish older than age four inhabit deeper waters (Rooker, Holt, and Holt 1998). Adults in the Gulf of America (TX) consume white shrimp, gulf menhaden, and blue crab. Dominant prey species shift seasonally (shrimp in fall, menhaden in spring) while some prey species are consistent throughout the year (crab) (Scharf and Schlicht 2000). Age at maturity in red drum off North Carolina is 3-4 years, with a maximum age of 62 years (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024). Red drum move out of estuaries into coastal waters as they mature. Seasonal movements with seasonal site fidelity have been observed in Georgia but some populations (particularly in the southern stock) are resident year round. Seasonal migrations between Virginia and North Carolina have been observed between May and September (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024). The northern stock is managed by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. The northern stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring, however the southern stock is overfished and overfishing is currently occurring (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024).

title: 31  Scup
section: 32.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
32.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Scup distributions in the spring and fall have shifted northward and offshore, which may necessitate new management strategies (Bell et al. 2015) (Palacios-Abrantes et al. 2023). Roday et al. (2026) analyzed fish distribution shifts using recreational catch and fishery independent data. Scup distributions showed a moderate range expansion but little to no change to center of distribution (Roday et al. 2026). Juvenile scup exposed to elevated pCO2 in a laboratory setting had no significant effect on growth, survival, or otolith condition. Scup spawning areas in Long Island Sound are already exposed to high pCO2, indicating some tolerance to ocean acidification (Perry et al. 2015).

title: 31  Scup
text:
32.8 Life History Synopsis:
Scup are found in the Northwest Atlantic from Cape Cod, Massachusetts to Cape Hatteras, North Carolina (Steimle et al. 1999). There are two U.S. stocks: Mid-Atlantic/New England (Atlantic coast) and South Atlantic (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025). Spawning occurs once per year in coastal bays and estuaries in May-August, beginning with lower latitudes (Steimle et al. 1999). Eggs take 2-3 days to hatch depending on temperature. The larval stage lasts from 2 mm TL to 18-19 TL; the duration of this period is unclear. Larval distributions are highest in inshore bays and estuaries, suggesting that eggs and larvae do not travel far during planktonic stages (Steimle et al. 1999). The biggest cue for larval settlement is size (~25 mm), with size in part being a function of temperature - most common at 15-20°C (Steimle et al. 1999). Larval diets are poorly understood, but larvae have been successfully reared on zooplankton in lab experiments (Bowman et al. 1987). Early juveniles consume copepods, so larval scup may also consume copepods (Michelman 1988). Larval predators include plantivorous medusae, crustaceans, and fishes (Steimle et al. 1999). Juveniles have been caught in intertidal and subtidal habitats, over sand, silty-sand, shell, mud, mussel beds and eelgrass (Zosteria marina). Offshore winter-spring distributions indicate that they occur in habitats ranging from relatively flat, open, sandy-silty bottoms to the head of submarine canyons, and other areas with topographical relief and varying sediments. Juveniles can use biogenic depressions, sand wave troughs, and possibly mollusk shell fields for shelter in winter, suggesting structure may be important to juveniles (Able and Fahay 1998). Juveniles primarily feed on polychaetes, epibenthic amphipods and other small crustaceans, mollusks, and fish eggs and larvae (Steimle et al. 1999). Prey size increases with individual size, with copepods and mysids being important to early juveniles and bivalve mollusks consumed by larger fish (Bowman et al. 1987). Adult scup are also benthic feeders and forage on a variety of prey, including small crustaceans (including zooplankton), polychaetes, mollusks, small squid, vegetable detritus, insect larvae, hydroids, sand dollars, amphipods, and small fish (Steimle et al. 1999). Similar to juveniles, their prey size scales with individual size. Diets can vary based on location, and season. Feeding slows or stops entirely in winter (Byron and Link 2010). Adults are primarily benthic and utilize both estuarine and shelf benthos (Steimle et al. 1999). Age at maturity is around 2 years of age, with a maximum age between 15-20 years (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2015). Scup undertake extensive inshore/offshore migrations driven by temperature preferences. They migrate south and offshore in the fall and return to inshore bays and estuaries in the spring to spawn. Scup can become inactive or dormant if caught in cold pockets < 6°C during their inshore spring migration and prefer temperatures 9-24°C (Steimle et al. 1999). The North Atlantic scup fishery is managed jointly by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council and Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. The 2025 Management Track Assessment reported the Atlantic coast stock as not overfished and overfishing not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025).

title: 32  Shortfin squid
section: 33.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
33.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Distribution and abundance of shortfin squid on the Northeast U.S. Continental Shelf is highly related to oceanographic conditions such as shelf-slope dynamics and positions of the shelf-slope front and Gulf Stream (Dawe et al. 2007). Dawe, Colbourne, and Drinkwater (2000) theorized based on catch data from Newfoundland that squid abundance may co-vary with that of its most important predators, competitors, and prey in an adaptive manner such that the population increases and expands when niche space becomes available. These shifts appear to result from high water temperatures, a negative North Atlantic Oscillation (NAO) index, and a southward shift in the Gulf Stream position. Greater catch per unit effort (CPUE) of shortfin squid has been associated with cooler bottom temperatures, timing and location of warm core rings in the winter and spring, and slope water/shelf slope front dynamics (Salois et al. 2023). These features may contribute to areas of high primary productivity, food sources, and transport mechanisms for larvae.

title: 32  Shortfin squid
text:
33.8 Life History Synopsis:
The northern shortfin squid inhabits Northwest Atlantic shelf and slope waters from the Labrador Sea to the Florida Straits (Hendrickson and Holmes 2004). Spawning occurs year round, but peaks seasonally from October to June. Shortfin squid are semelparous; females release multiple egg capsules during a single spawning season, after which they die (Hendrickson and Holmes 2004). Laboratory studies indicate that hatching occurs in 16 days at 13°C, 12 days at 16°C, and 8 days at 21°C. In the laboratory, paralarvae enter a transitional stage at approximately 5.0 mm ML, followed by a juvenile stage at about 7.0 mm ML (O’Dor et al. 1982). The Gulf Stream and associated eddies are hypothesized to be the primary mechanisms for transport of larvae to northern latitudes and then inshore via the eddies (Trites 1983). Larvae have small yolk-sacs, so they rely on rapid transportation to food-rich habitats (Hendrickson 2004). Larvae feed on a variety of prey items (fish, nematodes, euphausiids). Larvae and juveniles are abundant in the slope water of the Gulf Stream and in the adjacent upwelling regions at times of maximum production such as the spring plankton bloom (Hendrickson and Holmes 2004). As the initial biomass of the bloom is transferred up through various trophic levels, the squid grow at a pace which allows them to feed continuously at the level where biomass is peaking (O’Dor and Dawe 2013). Paralarvae, juveniles and adults of Illex exhibit diel vertical migrations to feed at night, and remain near the seabed during the day (Arkhipkin and Fedulov 1986). An ontogenetic shift in diet from a predominance of crustaceans to a predominance of fish and squid is evident as the squid mature (Dawe 1988). Larger, older Northern shortfin squid feed primarily on fish, but cannibalism of small individuals (most likely males) by larger females also occurs, particularly during autumn. Fish prey consists of the early life history stages of Atlantic cod, Arctic cod, redfish, sand lance, mackerel, Atlantic herring, haddock, and sculpin (Dawe 1988). Shortfin squid are important prey for seabirds and many fish species, including tunas, hakes, spiny dogfish, monkfish, and Atlantic cod (Hendrickson and Holmes 2004). Juveniles and adults make seasonal migrations off the continental shelf in the fall and back onto the shelf to spawning grounds in the Mid-Atlantic in the spring (Hendrickson and Holmes 2004). Shortfin squid are a short lived species, reaching maturity at 40-60 days and a maximum lifespan of less than one year (Hendrickson 2004). Adults are found at bottom temperatures between 3.5-15°C and surface temperatures greater than 20°C (Hendrickson and Holmes 2004). Shortfin squid in the Northwest Atlantic are managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council under the Mackerel, Squid, and Butterfish Fishery Management Plan. The most recent stock assessment occurred in 2025; management measures are in place but stock status is unknown as there are no reference points for this stock (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2025).

title: 33  Smooth dogfish
section: 34.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
34.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Little information exists on the potential effects of climate on smooth dogfish abundance and distribution. Coastal areas, including estuaries and smooth dogfish nursing grounds, are affected by anthropogenic activities that degrade a portion of smooth dogfish habitat. These areas are also more likely to be impacted by hypoxia and changes to freshwater input/increases in turbidity (Plumlee et al. 2018). Smooth dogfish exposed to higher temperatures (increase of 6°C) did not result in a thermal stress response, indicating a possible tolerance and ability for range expansion (Bockus et al. 2020).

title: 33  Smooth dogfish
text:
34.8 Life History Synopsis:
Smooth dogfish are a demersal, highly migratory shark species (HMS). The Northwest Atlantic population of smooth dogfish ranges from Cape Cod to Florida and the northern Gulf of America (Conrath, Gelsleichter, and Musick 2002). The species undergoes temperature-driven seasonal migrations north in the summer and south in the winter. They overwinter in Chesapeake Bay to South Carolina from January to May, migrating north to Southern New England beginning in early spring to give birth to pups (Castro 2010) (Dell’Apa et al. 2018). Females ovulate between June and July, with gestation lasting 10 months. Birth occurs in southern New England between early May to mid-July (Castro 2010). Once females have given birth in nearshore or estuarine areas,, young of year and immature age classes may remain in nursery grounds while females migrate north to suitable temperatures (Bangley and Rulifson 2017). Smooth dogfish prefer temperatures 9-32°C. They begin appearing in the mid-Atlantic and New England when temperatures are greater than 6-7°C, and are most abundant along the East Coast between 13-15°C (Bockus et al. 2020). Young of year individuals use shallow shoals and marsh creek habitats particularly during night hours. Juveniles are found throughout temperate coastal estuaries, creeks, and seagrass beds, which may serve as nursery grounds (Castro 2010). Juveniles prey on small shrimp, polychaete worms, and crabs, and may fill an important predatory role during times of year when other piscivores are rare (Rountree and Able 1996). Adult smooth dogfish are bottom feeders, primarily consuming crustaceans and mainly feeling at night. They are described as a trophic specialist due to the morphological and functional modifications that allow them to feed on hard-shelled/exoskeleton-bearing prey (Castro 2010) (Bangley and Rulifson 2017). Smooth dogfish are prey items for a variety of larger sharks and fishes (Gelsleichter, Musick, and Nichols 1999). Age at maturity is 2-3 years for males and 4-7 years for females, with a maximum age of 11 years for males and 13 years for females (Conrath, Gelsleichter, and Musick 2002) (Giresi et al. 2013). In state waters, smooth dogfish are managed by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission’s Interstate Fishery Management Plan for Coastal Sharks (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024). NOAA Fisheries produces an annual Stock Assessment and Fisheries Evaluation (SAFE) Report detailing the status of HMS in federal waters (National Marine Fisheries Service 2023). The current stock status is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Southeast Data, Assessment, and Review 2015).

title: 34  Softshell clam
section: 35.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
35.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Softshell clams are sensitive to increasing temperatures; temperature influences growth and reproduction, and their southern range boundary is controlled by sea surface temperatures (V. Kennedy and Mihursky 1971). Population declines in softshell clams in the Wadden Sea result from thermal stress caused by high water temperatures (Freitas et al. 2007). Softshell clams are likely to be vulnerable to ocean acidification, impacting their behavior, physiology, growth, and survival (Clements and Hunt 2023). Koo et al. (2017) described salinity accounting for the majority of spatial variation in softshell clam growth in Plum Island Sound, Massachusetts. Salinity gradients in Northwest Atlantic estuaries are expected to alter with sea level rise and increasing precipitation, which may prompt management concerns (Koo et al. 2017).

title: 34  Softshell clam
text:
35.8 Life History Synopsis:
The softshell clam is a circumboreal species native to the Northwest Atlantic from Labrador to Cape Hatteras (Newell, Hidu, and Parsons 1986). Softshell clams reach maturity in 1-4 years, depending on the length of each growing season. The typical lifespan is between 10-12 years, but cases have been recorded of some softshell clams reaching 28 years of age (Cohen 2005). These clams can be found at temperatures as low as 2°C but prefer between 10-28°C (Newell, Hidu, and Parsons 1986) (Landry 2021). Spawning occurs between 4-25°C, with optimal temperatures between 10-15°C (Abraham and Dillon 1986). Fecundity also increases with size and age, particularly with females (Cohen 2005). Separate male and female sexes occur in the species, with fertilization occurring externally in the environment. Female softshell clams can release from 1-5 million eggs in their lifetime and typically one to two spawnings happen a year, one in early spring and the other in late summer, depending on locality and monthly tidal cycles (Abraham and Dillon 1986). Approximately 12 hours after fertilization, larvae hatch from the eggs. Larvae spend 2-5 weeks free-floating with plankton and other organic particles before metamorphosizing and settling to the bottom of the sea floor. The clams then spend another 2-5 weeks moving along the sea floor or temporarily attaching themselves to the surface of objects (Cohen 2005). Food availability and optimal oceanographic conditions to help transport and/or retain eggs and larvae in suitable habitats help drive local recruitment (Bowen and Hunt 2009). Eggs and larvae are preyed upon by a variety of predators, including fish and other filtering invertebrates. High fecundity likely reduces the impact of predation on egg and larvae (Newell, Hidu, and Parsons 1986). Metamorphosis and settlement to the benthos can be delayed in favor of more suitable substrates, and may be driven by chemical cues (Snelgrove, Grant, and Pilditch 1999). Once settled, softshell clams spend 2-5 weeks moving along the sea floor or temporarily attaching themselves to the surface of objects. They begin to burrow into the sediment in brackish waters, estuaries, and marine habitats, and stay burrowed for the rest of their life (Cohen 2005). Burrows are constructed 20-30cm below the sediment surface and in waters up to 200m depth. Sandy sediment, with a sandy-mud mixture or with some gravel, is preferred (Tyler-Walters 2003). The softshell clam is an active suspension feeder, filtering organic particles and microinvertebrates such as plankton, filamentous algae, diatoms, jellyfish and fish larvae, and flagellates (Tyler-Walters 2003). Predation plays a large role in juvenile and adult distribution and survival. Predators include birds, fish, shrimp, worms, crabs, and snails (Newell, Hidu, and Parsons 1986). The green crab, an invasive species, is one of the more destructive clam predators and declines in clam production have been linked to increases in green crab abundance. Blue crabs are also major predators in Chesapeake Bay; exclusion studies have illustrated that recruitment and survival increases when blue crabs are excluded (Landry 2021). The softshell clam supports important state-managed commercial and recreational fisheries, and are harvested with rakes, forks, or hydraulic dredges (Newell, Hidu, and Parsons 1986). Recent declines in commercial landings can be attributed to overfishing, habitat loss, pollution, and climate change. However, mariculture in softshell clams has been successful in building populations for restoration or commercial fishing (V. S. Kennedy and Beal 2023).

title: 35  Spanish mackerel
section: 36.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
36.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Little work exists on the effects of climate on Spanish mackerel populations in the Northwest Atlantic. Temperature likely limits movement; they are not found in waters colder than 18°C (Berrien and Finan 1977). Rising ocean temperatures may expand suitable habitat in the Mid-Atlantic and north to New England. Larval Spanish mackerel were largely resistant to contaminants from the Deepwater Horizon oil spill, and larval abundance did not differ before v. after the spill (Ransom, Filbrun, and Hernandez Jr 2016).

title: 35  Spanish mackerel
text:
36.8 Life History Synopsis:
Spanish mackerel are a migratory, schooling fish found along the eastern United States and Gulf from New York to Florida (Godcharles and Murphy 1986). There are two Spanish mackerel stocks, one in the South Atlantic and one in the Gulf of America (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2022) (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2023). The Atlantic stock overwinters off the Florida coast and migrates northward to the Mid-Atlantic in the spring and early summer. They return south to Florida once the Mid-Atlantic cools in the fall (Berrien and Finan 1977). Spanish mackerel prefer temperatures 21-27°C and are not seen below 18°C (Berrien and Finan 1977). Spawning occurs from May to September along the southeast Atlantic coast, with lower latitudes spawning earlier than higher latitudes (Collins and Stender 1987). In Chesapeake Bay, spawning occurs from June to August, peaking in June (Cooksey 1996). Females are batch spawners, releasing as many as 1.5 million eggs over the spawning season (Collins and Stender 1987). Larval collection data indicate that spawning occurs at depths of 12-35 meters over the inner continental shelf including estuarine and coastal areas (McEachran, Finucane, and Hall 1980). Hatching occurs approximately 25 hours after fertilization at water temperatures averaging 26ºC. Larvae and early juveniles grow 1.9 mm per day for approximately the first 23 days of life. From 23-40 days, growth is accelerated, with young fishes growing as much as 5 mm per day. Thereafter, growth slows to approximately 2.1 mm per day (Schmidt, Collins, and Wyanski 1993). Larvae are found in surface waters between 19.6–29.8°C with a salinity of 28.3–37.4 ppt. It is unclear if there is a dependency on currents for transport to nursery habitats (Godcharles and Murphy 1986). Larvae feed on other larval fishes such as carngids, clupeids, and engraulids and crustaceans (Berrien and Finan 1977). Juveniles and adults are voracious, opportunistic, carnivores. Food consists mostly of small fishes with lesser quantities of pandalid and penaeoid shrimps and squids. Clupeoids such as round herring, thread herring, menhaden, and alewives, along with anchovies, are important forage species. They seldom feed on bottom invertebrates (Bowman et al. 2000) (Berrien and Finan 1977). Spanish mackerel reach maturity around 1-1.5 years of age (Juan-Jord’a et al. 2013) with a maximum lifespan of around 12 years (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2022). They are largely preyed upon by dolphins, sharks, and tunas (Berrien and Finan 1977). Management of Spanish mackerel in state waters is coordinated by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC) Coastal Pelagics Management Board. Both stocks are not overfished or subject to overfishing (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2022) (SouthEast Data, Assessment, and Review (SEDAR) 2023).

title: 36  Spiny dogfish
section: 37.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
37.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Nye et al. (2009) observed a range expansion and southward shift in center of biomass in spiny dogfish, although changes in biomass over the study period may have influenced these shifts. Seasonal occurrence of spiny dogfish is highly correlated with depth and bottom temperature, with a higher probability of occurrence in warmer years versus colder years (Sagarese, Frisk, Cerrato, et al. 2014). Spiny dogfish shift their distribution seasonally; variation in environmental drivers may impact population and fishery dynamics as temperatures continue to increase. Climate-inducing seasonal distribution shifts influencing habitat availability for spiny dogfish may also lead to vulnerability of the inshore fishery in the fall (Sagarese, Frisk, Miller, et al. 2014).

title: 36  Spiny dogfish
text:
37.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic spiny dogfish are a schooling, highly migratory shark species (HMS) inhabiting inshore and offshore waters from Labrador to Florida, although they are most abundant from Nova Scotia and Cape Hatteras. Spiny dogfish perform diel vertical migrations; most schools are found around 40-50 m depth, but can descend upwards of 500 m (Stehlik 2007) (Carlson et al. 2014). There are populations that occupy the continental shelves of Europe, Asia, and the northeast Pacific, but these populations do not mix (Stehlik 2007). Spiny dogfish perform north-south migrations and move between inshore-offshore waters in response to water temperature, migrating north in the spring and summer and south in the fall and winter to remain in waters between 6-13°C (Stehlik 2007) (Carlson et al. 2014). Age at maturity is 6 years for males and 12 years for females, with a lifespan of 35 years for males and 40 years for females. Spawning occurs offshore in the winter, with a gestation period of 18-24 months (Stehlik 2007). Proportions of mature females in catch are correlated with bottom temperatures, with more mature females caught at relatively higher bottom temperatures. However, how temperature impacts reproduction is still unclear (Sagarese, Frisk, Miller, et al. 2014). Spiny dogfish are opportunistic feeders, preying upon a variety of fishes (herring, mackerel, hakes, sand lance, menhaden), squid, ctenophores, polychaetes, crustaceans, bivalves. Diets can shift based on location and availability (Stehlik 2007) (DeVries, Gartland, and Latour 2025). Predators of spiny dogfish include sharks, seals, cod, goosefish, dolphins, and larger fish (Stehlik 2007). Spiny dogfish occupy a similar niche to that of Atlantic cod, whose populations have been impacted by increasing seal populations in the Northwest Atlantic and Canada. It has been hypothesized that the loss of cod could be a benefit to spiny dogfish, since they occupy similar feeding niches (Neuenhoff et al. 2019) (Ono et al. 2019). The Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) leads the joint management of the Spiny Dogfish Fishery Management Plan, with support from the New England Fishery Management Council (NEFMC) and Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC) (Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council (MAFMC) 2025). The ASMFC manages spiny dogfish in state waters via the Interstate Fishery Management Plan for Spiny Dogfish (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC) 2002). As of 2023, spiny dogfish in the Northwest Atlantic are not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2023).

title: 37  Striped bass
section: 38.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
38.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Coutant (1990) analyzed habitat suitability for striped bass on the U.S. east coast with predicted temperature increases and declines in precipitation. In freshwater areas, high temperatures, altered streamflow, and low oxygen may constrain suitable habitat in the summer. Habitat loss in the Chesapeake Bay is predicted, with a possible range expansion into Nova Scotia and further into the Gulf of St. Lawrence (Coutant 1990). Onset and cessation of the spawning season in two spawning grounds of the Hudson River Estuary have occurred earlier in recent years. Spawning sites in the upper estuary are more vulnerable to climate change compared to sites in the lower estuary (Pan et al. 2023). Larval striped bass abundance in the Hudson River Estuary is positively correlated with high freshwater flows, which may decrease under predicted climate scenarios (O’Connor et al. 2012). Striped bass recruitment has been linked to winter North Atlantic Oscillation (NAO) and marine heatwaves (Dixon et al. 2026). Several studies support a general conclusion that dominant year classes through recent history have always been preceded by a colder than normal winter (i.e. subnormal winter temperatures). One proposed mechanism of the severe winter-strong year class association is through the estuarine food chain (Heinle and Flemer 1975) (Heinle, Flemer, and Ustach 1977).

title: 37  Striped bass
text:
38.8 Life History Synopsis:
Atlantic striped bass inhabit freshwater coastal and estuarine waters from Canada to Florida, including the Gulf of America from Florida to Louisiana (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2019). Striped bass are anadromous, spawning once a year in fresh or nearly fresh water. Along the Atlantic coast, the spawning period ranges from mid-February in Florida to June and July in the Gulf of St. Lawrence. In the mid-Atlantic, spawning is concentrated during the months of April - June (Bigelow and Schroeder 1953). Spawning peaks are apparently triggered by a noticeable increase in water temperature, generally beginning at temperatures of at least 14°C (Johnson and Koo 1975). Eggs hatch within 30 - 74 hrs, occurring faster at higher temperatures. Larval stages last up until age 1 when juveniles migrate out of rivers and into estuaries (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2019). Larvae and eggs may drift down stream in rivers, away from spawning sites. Eggs are semi-buoyant, requiring a current of 30 cm/sec in order to keep the eggs afloat so they don’t settle to the bottom and die (Fay, Neves, and Pardue 1983). Egg and larval mortality, as well as larval growth rates and yolk utilization are all linked to water temperatures greater than 18°C (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2019). Predation and competition by American shad are important factors in spawning and larval locations (Fay, Neves, and Pardue 1983). In addition, adult and juvenile white perch and copepod Cyclops bicuspidatus probably consume large numbers of striped bass larvae (Smith and Kernehan 1981). Larval striped bass feed on zooplankton; juveniles eat small shrimps and other crustaceans, annelid worms, bay anchovies, gobies, and insects (Bason 1971). Most juveniles remain in the river and estuarine areas where they were spawned. During the first summer, young-of-the-year striped bass generally move downstream and shoreward (Fay, Neves, and Pardue 1983). A variety of environmental factors influence the timing of this migration, including temperature, pH, and salinity (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2019). Males reach maturity at age 2-4 and females from age 4-8. Striped bass have a fairly long lifespan, up to 30 years (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2019). Adults are generalists, consuming a variety of fish and invertebrates, depending on fish size, time of year, and habitat (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2019). Adults have few predators, with the exception of some seals and sharks (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2019). The Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission manages striped bass in coastal and estuarine waters from Maine to Virginia, and coastal areas of North Carolina under Amendment 7 of the Interstate Fishery Management Plan (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2022). Estuarine waters of North Carolina are managed by the state. The stock is currently overfished, with management measures and a rebuilding plan in place, but not subject to overfishing (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2024).

title: 38  Summer flounder
section: 39.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
39.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Summer flounder distributions are correlated with depth, bottom temperature, sea surface temperature (SST), and bottom salinity (Deen, Jauss, and Sullivan 2025). Their distribution in the Northwest Atlantic may shifting north due to increasing winter temperatures, but could also be affected by reductions in fishing pressure or range expansion (Bell et al. 2014). Large-scale climate drivers, such as the Gulf Stream Index, may affect summer flounder recruitment and distribution (O’Leary et al. 2019). Spatio-temporal modeling results from (Deen, Jauss, and Sullivan 2025) report increased abundance of summer flounder in the fall by 10-15% with a 1°C increase in SST, 12% with a 1°C increase in bottom temperature and 3-4% increase with each meter in depth. In the spring, abundance increased by 30% with a 1°C increase in SST. Summer flounder embryos exhibit reduced survival when exposed to elevated CO2. Larvae exposed to elevated CO2 had tissue damage and initiated metamorphosis earlier and at smaller sizes (Chambers et al. 2014).

title: 38  Summer flounder
text:
39.8 Life History Synopsis:
Summer flounder are found both inshore and offshore in waters from Nova Scotia to Florida (Packer et al. 1999). Spawning season lasts from September through February/March, with lower latitudes starting sooner. Spawning adults migrate to overwintering habitats on the continental shelf edge. In the late spring, they move back inshore into shallow waters and estuaries (Packer et al. 1999). The timing of peak spawning in October/November coincides with the breakdown of thermal stratification on the continental shelf and the maximum production of autumn plankton which is characteristic of temperate ocean waters of the northern hemisphere (Packer et al. 1999) (Morse 1981). Egg development takes anywhere from 48-142 hours, depending on water temperatures. At 14°C, larvae metamorphosize into juveniles within approximately 65 days of hatching. Time to metamorphosis also was dependent on temperatures, with metamorphosis completing as fast as 46.5 days at 14.5°C (Smith 1973). Larvae do not drift far from spawning regions; however, tidal stream transport and vertical migration behavior may be important to determining larval distributions (Packer et al. 1999) (Hare, Walsh, and Wuenschel 2006). Larvae prefer waters with warmer temperatures and intermediate salinities. Temperature affects the rate of larvae yolk sac absorption (which impacts when larvae have to depend on exogenous feeding), development and growth (Watanabe et al. 1998). Studies have also shown that larval size increases with greater light intensity (presumably affecting activity and energy metabolism) and higher salinities (Watanabe et al. 1998) (Burke et al. 1998). Larvae are preyed upon by a variety of predators, including blue crabs and sea robins. The type and abundance of predators (ex: Crangon septemspinosa or pelagic mummichugs) could determine whether a metamorphic summer flounder stays in the substrate or the water column (C. septemspinosa would force them to the water column, and vice versa for mummichugs) (Keefe and Able 1994). Larvae preferentially settle on sandy substrate; bottom type, presence of predators, and water temperatures may all impact settlement (Keefe and Able 1994). Young-of-the-year can occur in a variety of habitats, including shallow, mud-bottomed marsh creeks, shallow sand substrates (including seagrass beds), deep sand substrate, and deep fine-sand substrates (Szedlmayer and Able 1993). Juveniles are opportunistic feeders and differences in diet are often related to the availability of prey. There also appears to be ontogenetic changes in diet. Smaller individuals eat polychaetes and crustaceans. Metamorphic stage individuals reflect calanoid copepod diets (pelagic), while late metamorphosis stage individual and juveniles’ diets reflected shrimp, polychaetes, harpactoid copepods, amphipods, small fishes, small gastropod mollusks, and plant material (benthic) (Grover 1998). Adults are demersal, with highest abundance in waters deeper than 30m and at temperatures 2-27°C (Packer et al. 1999). Adults are also opportunistic feeders. Fish and crustaceans make up the majority of their diet, but dominant prey items shift following prey availability (Cernadas-Martı́n et al. 2021). Adult summer flounder are preyed upon by sharks, rays, and monkfish (Packer et al. 1999). Summer flounder reach maturity at approximately 13-17 months. Males can reach 12 years of age and females 14 years, although individuals living upwards of 20 years have been reported (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2019). The summer flounder stock is cooperatively managed by the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council and Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission under the Summer Founder, Scup, and Black Sea Bass Fishery Management Plan. The stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025).

title: 39  Tautog
section: 40.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
40.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Temperature is linked to recruitment, spawning, and migration timing in tautog (Steimle and Shaheen 1999), but it is unclear how warming temperatures will affect phenology. Tautog exhibit some site fidelity, which may present a limited ability to expand or shift their range in response to warming temperatures (Langan et al. 2021). Moran and Gerry (2023) observed differences in prey type and feeding behavior in tautog exposed to varying temperatures, highlighting the need for further research on the relationship between increasing temperature and feeding behavior. (Howell and Simpson 1994) reported that tautog are not tolerant of dissolved oxygen (DO) concentrations below 2 mg/l, and prefer DO levels above 3 mg/l. Tautog mortalities were reported by commercial fishers and were observed on the beach during a major anoxia-hypoxia episode off New Jersey in 1976.

title: 39  Tautog
text:
40.8 Life History Synopsis:
Tautog are a slow-growing, long-lived coastal species inhabiting the outer coast of Nova Scotia to Georgia, although they are most abundant from Cape Cod to Chesapeake Bay (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). They do not reach maturity until age 3 (males) or 4 (females) and can live up to 25-30 years (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Tautog spawn in pairs, or in small groups with a single female and several males. The method depends on sex-ratios, the presence of a patriarchy, and availability of shelter and food (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). They spawn from April to August; occurring earlier (April) in the southern portion of the range. Peak spawning occurs in June/July in northern portions of habitat. They perform inshore/offshore migrations in order to spawn. In the north, tautog overwinter offshore and migrate inshore in the spring to spawn and feed before moving back offshore as temperatures decline (below 11°C) in the fall and winter. Southern populations may undertake shorter seasonal migrations or remain either inshore or offshore for the entire year (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Tautog enter a torpor state in waters between 7-10°C and prefer temperatures 10-22°C (Olla, Bejda, and Martin 1974). In a laboratory setting, eggs hatch in 42-48 hours at 20-22°C with longer development times at lower temperatures (Perry 1994). Larval duration lasts about 20-25 days, depending on location (Sogard 1992). Larvae consume small fish and copepods, such as Acartia tonsa (Simpson 1989). Larvae survival is dependent on temperatures for both incubation period and recruitment. There was no direct evidence in the literature to support reliance on specific hydrographic conditions, however, spawning locations near the mouths of nursery habitats,and possible offshore advection suggest that local current patterns may play a role in larval distribution (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Juveniles prefer areas with high amounts of cover that they can hide in/around. However, they can utilize a variety of vertical habitats, including piers, artificial and real seagrass/algal beds, shells, and marine debris. Larger juvenile and adult tautog are closely associated with hard- surface, reef-like habitats, remaining close to their preferred home location (Sogard 1992). Juvenile tautog forage on benthic, as well as planktonic, prey. As they grow, juvenile tautog rely less on planktonic food resources and feed primarily on benthic prey, although the prey remain primarily crustaceans, such as decapods (Dorf 1994). Adult tautog are durophagous and feed chiefly on blue mussels and other shellfish throughout the year (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Seabirds, particularly cormorants, prey on juvenile tautog. Hakes, smooth and spiny dogfish, skates, and sea ravens have been reported to consume adult tautog (Steimle and Shaheen 1999). Tautog is managed by the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission. The Tautog Fishery Management Plan divides the stock into four regions: Massachusetts-Rhode Island (MARI), Long Island Sound (LIS), New Jersey-New York Bight (NJ-NYB), and Delaware-Maryland-Virginia (DMV) (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2017). The 2025 stock assessment update indicated tautog are not overfished in MARI, LIS, or NJ-NYB, but overfished in DMV. Overfishing is not occurring in MARI or LIS, but the NJ-NYB and DMV regions are experiencing overfishing (Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission 2025).

title: 40  White hake
section: 41.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
41.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
White hake are a common groundfish species in the Gulf of Maine, which is warming faster than most of the world’s oceans (Pershing et al. 2021). In response to warming in the Gulf of Maine, white hake show a preference for narrow temperature (9-13°C) and depth (55-100 m) ranges and display a gradual northward movement in their center of gravity (LaFreniere et al. 2023). Nye et al. (2009) observed a range contraction in white hake in the Gulf of Maine. White hake may be affected by changes in circulation patterns. Small white hake larvae have been collected in early summer in a Gulf Stream warm core ring seaward of southern Scotian Shelf and southeast of Georges Bank. Patterns of strong recruitment have been linked to the strength of the Labrador Current on the Great Banks and strong onshore transport and downwelling conditions in coastal Newfoundland (Han and Kulka 2008) (Ings, Gregory, and Schneider 2008).

title: 40  White hake
text:
41.8 Life History Synopsis:
White hake are a groundfish species inhabiting deep water from Newfoundland to the Mid-Atlantic Bight (Chang, Morse, and Berrien 1999). Males reach maturity at 2.5 years and females at 2.8 years; both males and females have a lifespan of around 20 years of age (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2013). In the Gulf of Maine, white hake of all ages tend to migrate inshore in warmer months and disperse into deeper waters in colder months, while hake in the south move offshore to spawn in the spring; fall movements may be linked to prey movements (Langton, Pearce, and Gibson 1994). Based on the distribution and abundance of pelagic juveniles, as well as circulation patterns throughout the region, Fahay and Able (1989) suggested that the southern portion of the stock (below the Gulf of St. Lawrence) spawns in early spring (April-May) in deep waters along the continental slope, primarily off southern Georges Bank and the Mid-Atlantic Bight. White hake eggs are buoyant, and hatch in the water column in 3-7 days (Fahay 1983). Larvae may be affected by oceanographic conditions like warm core rings, the Gulf Stream, and Labrador Current for transport (Han and Kulka 2008) (Ings, Gregory, and Schneider 2008). White hake prefer temperatures between 7-20°C (Chang, Morse, and Berrien 1999). Eggs, larvae, and early juveniles are pelagic; older juveniles and adults are demersal. Most catches occur in Canadian waters at depths between 175 to 375 m with no differences between mature and immature hake. In the Gulf of Maine, depth preferences are 55-100 m (Fahay and Able 1989) (Nozères et al. 2015). Juvenile white hake settle to the bottom at around 2 months of age. Pelagic juveniles are in the upper water column during the daytime. Demersal juveniles inhabit muddy and fine grained sandy bottom sediment (Chang, Morse, and Berrien 1999) (Fahay and Able 1989). Demersal juveniles feed primarily on polychaetes, shrimps, and other crustaceans (Bowman 1981). Adults feed on fish, including juveniles of their own and similar species (silver hake), Atlantic herring, Gulf Stream flounder, and possibly alewife, shrimps, krill, and other crustaceans (Langton, Pearce, and Gibson 1994) (Nozères et al. 2015). The white hake fishery is managed as a single stock by the New England Fisheries Management Council, under the Northeast Multispecies (Small-Mesh) Fishery Management Plan. The stock is under a rebuilding plan, but is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center 2025).
