title: 41  Windowpane flounder
section: 42.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
42.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Percent biomass of windowpane flounder in the Northwest Atlantic increases with changes in seasonality, from cold springs, to warm springs, to fall (Dunn, Moxley, and Halpin 2016). Increasing temperatures (> 7-10°C) have been shown to displace windowpane flounder (Bell et al. 2021). In New York/New Jersey Harbor, cold winter temperatures decreased windowpane abundance and overwinter mortality in small juveniles (Wilber et al. 2016). Overwintering success appears to be driven in part by temperatures, with extreme cold conditions resulting in high overwinter mortality of age-0 individuals. This likely has a larger impact on fall-spawned juveniles since resilience to extreme cold temperatures is size-dependent and fall-spawned individuals have less time to grow (Neuman and Able 2009). Individual windowpane have also demonstrated avoiding hypoxic conditions (Cadrin et al. 2021).

title: 41  Windowpane flounder
text:
42.8 Life History Synopsis:
Windowpane flounder inhabit shallow waters from the Gulf of St. Lawrence to Florida, but are most abundant from Georges Bank to Chesapeake Bay (Chang et al. 1999). They reach maturity around 1.5 years of age and can live up to 8 years (Cadrin et al. 2021). Windowpane spawn from February to November on the inner continental shelf, Georges Bank, and within estuaries. Spawning occurs in the Mid-Atlantic Bight in both spring and fall, whereas spawning in Georges Bank peaks in summer (Able and Fahay 2010) (Cadrin et al. 2021). Windowpane spawn in the evening or at night on or near bottom at temperatures ranging from 6-21°C (Chang et al. 1999). Eggs hatch after 8 days. The duration of the larval stage is unknown, but eggs and larvae remain in the water column feeding on suspended copepods (Chang et al. 1999). Surveys from Canadian waters suggest that small (< 10 cm TL) windowpane consume bay shrimp and crab larvae, which may be a transition point from zooplankton prey to larger prey items (Hanson and Wilson 2014). Larvae settle to the bottom at approximately 10 mm TL. However, individuals collected on Georges Bank may be planktonic up to 20 mm. There is no indication that settlement is driven by environmental factors (Morse and Able 1995). Juveniles that settle in shallow inshore waters move to deeper offshore waters as they grow. Juveniles inhabiting Georges Bank (< 60 m) undergo seasonal movements to deeper waters along the southern flank of the Bank occur during late autumn, as bottom temperatures drop, and overwintering occurs in deeper areas until late spring (Klein-MacPhee 2002). From Massachusetts to the Gulf of Maine, they can be found in estuaries and on the shelf (Chang et al. 1999). Adults are found on the benthos in waters < 100 m and tolerate a wide range of temperatures (0-26.8°C) (Chang et al. 1999). Adult windowpane may travel along the coast for considerable distances. Juveniles and adults may migrate to nearshore or estuarine habitats in the Mid-Atlantic Bight. Seasonal movements into offshore overwintering habitats have also been documented (Chang et al. 1999). Stomach content data collected during Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) bottom trawl surveys indicate windowpane feed on small crustaceans (e.g., mysids and decapod shrimp) and various fish larvae including hakes and tomcod, as well as their own species (Langton and Bowman 1981) (Chang et al. 1999). Windowpane flounder are managed by the New England Fishery Management Council under the Northeast Multispecies (Groundfish) Fishery Management Plan. The fishery is divided into two stocks: Northern Gulf of Maine/Georges Bank and Southern New England/Mid-Atlantic. Overfishing and overfished status of the northern stock is unknown, as the assessment has not estimated reference points since 2020 (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2023a). The southern stock is not overfished and overfishing is not occurring (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2023b).

title: 42  Winter flounder
section: 43.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
43.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
A number of studies describe evidence of changes in winter flounder growth, abundance, and distribution as a result of climate change. The Southern New England/Mid-Atlantic Bight stock declined sharply in the 1980s from overfishing, has not recovered, and is shifting northward (Able et al. 2014) (Langan 2021). Warm spring temperatures have resulted in poor recruitment (Able et al. 2014), and declining productivity due to warming estuarine waters has blocked recovery efforts (Bell et al. 2014). Elevated water temperatures can cause faster development rates of early life history winter flounder; length of incubation time is crucial as a nearshore and estuarine species as these areas are more susceptible to anthropogenic impacts (Klein, Smith, and Kritzer 2017). Increasing temperatures may alter predation rates, affecting survival of larval winter flounder, as well as migration and distribution patterns (Klein, Smith, and Kritzer 2017). In Narragansett Bay, warmer summer temperatures were linked to increasing mortality in young-of-the-year winter flounder. Greater abundance of mature winter flounder in the lower bay during spring may also indicate a potential shift in spawning migration timing (Langan 2021).

title: 42  Winter flounder
text:
43.8 Life History Synopsis:
Winter flounder are found in estuaries and on the continental shelf from Nova Scotia to North Carolina, and are most common in the Mid-Atlantic north of Delaware Bay (Pereira 1999). They prefer temperatures between 0-20°C and depths less than 30m inshore and 100m offshore (Pereira 1999). The fishery is divided into 3 stocks: Gulf of Maine, Georges Bank, and Southern New England/Mid-Atlantic. They reach maturity at 2- 3 years, varying across stocks with southern populations maturing younger than northern ones, and live to a maximum age of 15 years (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2011). Winter flounder spawn inshore in the winter through spring, peaking in February/March in the Mid-Atlantic and Southern New England and continuing into May in the Gulf of Maine (Pereira 1999). Timing of spawning aggregations is linked to inshore migration timing, which is complex and possibly related to time of year, temperature, lunar cycle, and photoperiod, as well as varying across years. Individuals migrate inshore in the fall, and migrate offshore in the spring. The Georges Bank population does not migrate (Pearcy 1962). Females deposit eggs at night and on bottom substrates, usually sand or algal mats, and can deposit multiple batches of eggs per spawning season (Ziegler, Zacharias, and Frisk 2019). Eggs are demersal and hatch within 2-3 weeks, depending on temperature. Larvae are initially planktonic but become more demersal as they metamorphosize, which starts 5-6 weeks after hatching. Larvae rely on bottom currents to keep them in and around spawning areas in inshore bays and estuaries. They are tolerant to a wide range of temperatures, salinities, and dissolved oxygen concentrations (Pearcy 1962). Metamorphosis is complete about 8 weeks after hatching, and is temperature dependent (Pereira 1999). Size, rather than age, tends to drive metamorphosis and settlement (Manderson et al. 2003). Larvae consume a variety of planktonic prey including nauplii, invertebrate eggs, calanoids, and polychaetes, with larger larvae consuming larger prey. Copepods and other zooplankton are important during metamorphosis (Stehlik and Meise 2000). Small medusae are important predators to larvae. Larvae can also be preyed upon by amphipods and Atlantic mackerel (Pereira 1999). Juveniles utilize marine and estuarine rocky, sand, and mud substrates, particularly fine sand and gravel as well as marine and estuarine water columns. They also occupy submerged aquatic vegetation, marshes, oyster reefs, and macroalgae habitats (Bigelow and Schroeder 1953). Juveniles consume a variety of prey including copepods, amphipods, and polychaetes. The diversity of prey increases and the importance of smaller zooplankton decreases with size (Pearcy 1962) (Pereira 1999). Adults are omnivorous opportunistic feeders. They feed during the day as they depend on sight to locate prey. Their diet is limited by mouth size; diets primarily consist of polychaetes and crustaceans (mostly amphipods), but also include bivalves, fish eggs, and fish. Diets can shift based on available prey (Stehlik and Meise 2000) (Pereira 1999). Predators of adult winter flounder include striped bass, spiny dogfish, and seals (Pereira 1999). The New England Fishery Management Council manages winter flounder in federal waters under the Northeast Multispecies (Groundfish) Fishery Management Plan. The Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission coordinates management in state waters. None of the 3 stocks are subject to overfishing. The Georges Bank and Southern New England/Mid-Atlantic stocks are not overfished; the Gulf of Maine stock has not been assessed and population abundance is unknown (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025b) (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025c) (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025a).

title: 43  Witch flounder
section: 44.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
44.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
There have been few direct studies on the effect of climate change on witch flounder. Larval growth and survival has been linked to temperature in laboratory conditions (Bidwell and Howell 2001). Murawski (1993) included witch flounder in a list of species that exhibit low variation in seasonal temperature or depth of occurrence. Mills et al. (2024) found that witch flounder distribution has actually shifted significantly toward more southern latitudes.

title: 43  Witch flounder
text:
44.8 Life History Synopsis:
Witch flounder are a long-lived flatfish species inhabiting deep, cold waters in the Northwest Atlantic from Labrador to North Carolina (Cargnelli et al. 1999). They are slow-growing, reaching maturity at 4.5 years for males and 6 years for females in Georges Bank and the Gulf of Maine (Burnett, Ross, and Clark 1992). Witch flounder can live up to 30 years, reaching a maximum length of 78 cm (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Witch flounder spawn from March to November, with peak spawning occurring in summer. The general trend is for spawning to occur progressively later from south to north. In the Gulf of Maine-Georges Bank region, spawning occurs from April to November, and peaks from May to August (Martin and Drewry 1978) (Burnett, Ross, and Clark 1992). Powles and Kohler (1970) suggested that witch flounder occupy three largely discrete ecological niches, resulting in the different life stages being geographically separated from each other. The pelagic egg and larval stages are spent in the water column over deep water. Egg hatching occurs 7 to 8 days after spawning, with the yolk sac absorbed 10 days after hatching. Larvae are pelagic, and the larval stage lasts from 4-6 months to 1 year (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Eggs generally drift southward, and shelf currents may transport some larvae. Length may be the biggest cue for settlement (Cargnelli et al. 1999). The juvenile stage occurs in very deep water when metamorphosed fish settle to the bottom. They are found over mud, clay, silt, or muddy sand substrates (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Juveniles remain separated from the adult population, occupying deeper areas until the onset of sexual maturity. Walsh (1987) suggests however that while the juveniles and adults are in different areas, the niche separation may not be true. Witch flounder are capable of inhabiting depths up to 1500 m, although the majority are found at 90-300 m (Martin and Drewry 1978) (Klein-MacPhee 2002). The main food items in the witch flounder diet are polychaetes and crustaceans, although mollusks and echinoderms are also important. Overall, polychaetes were by far the most important food item, accounting for greater than 70% of the diet (Maurer and Bowman 1975). However, there is a distinct ontogenetic shift in diet, with polychaetes increasing in importance and crustaceans decreasing in importance with age (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Adult witch flounder generally do not migrate, although they prefer to stay within temperatures of 4-11°C. Some subpopulations may move from shallower waters in warmer months to deeper overwintering waters. Migration was not observed in the Gulf of Maine (Cargnelli et al. 1999). Predators of witch flounder include monkfish, spiny dogfish, harp seals, Atlantic halibut, white hake, and some skates (Klein-MacPhee 2002). Witch flounder are managed as a single stock unit by the New England Fishery Management Council under the Northeast Multispecies (Groundfish) Fishery Management Plan. In the last Management Track Assessment, the stock status could not be determined due to a lack of biological reference points. The stock condition appears to be improving but is poor relative to historical levels (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2024).

title: 44  Yellowtail flounder
section: 45.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
text:
45.7 Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:
Yellowtail flounder abundance, distribution, and recruitment have been associated with a number of environmental and climate indices. Rapid warming of the Northwest Atlantic has resulted in decreased productivity in yellow flounder (J. A. Kittel et al. 2026). Higher temperatures can increase mortality in yellowtail flounder larvae, and faster rates of warming causing earlier stratification may reduce settlement rates in juveniles (Klein, Smith, and Kritzer 2017). Yellowtail recruitment in Southern New England is highly correlated with the North Atlantic Oscillation (NAO) and air temperature (Sullivan, Cowen, and Steves 2005). Low recruitment since 1990 was associated with unfavorable environmental conditions caused by a northward shift in the Gulf Stream (Xu 2016) (J. Kittel 2025). The Mid-Atlantic Cold Pool, bottom temperature, and the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation (AMO) are also linked to recruitment (J. Kittel 2025). As warming continues, joint management between the United States and Canada may be more challenging as distributions change (Sumaila, Palacios-Abrantes, and Cheung 2020).

title: 44  Yellowtail flounder
text:
45.8 Life History Synopsis:
Yellowtail flounder are found in the Northwest Atlantic from Newfoundland to Chesapeake Bay, inhabiting sandy bottom waters between 130-230 m depth (Johnson et al. 1999) and at temperatures of -1 to 18°C (Overholtz and Cadrin 1998). Previous research has suggested that fish live up to 17 years, possibly as high as 20 years, although fish older than 7 years is unusual (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2012). The fishery is divided into 3 stocks: Gulf of Maine/Cape Cod, Georges Bank, and Southern New England/Mid-Atlantic (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025a) (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025b) (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025c). Most females are mature by ages 2-3 (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2012). Spawning occurs from mid-February to July along the continental shelf waters of Georges Bank, northwest of Cape Cod, Southern New England and nearshore along New Jersey and southern Long Island (Martin and Drewry 1978). Spawning peaks from April to June in southern New England and late June to July in Canada (Berrien and Sibunka 1999). Laboratory studies show eggs begin to hatch 6-7 days after fertilization (Benoit and Pepin 1999). At sea hatching times range from 14.5 days at 4⁰C to 4.5 days at 14⁰C (Walsh and Morgan 2004). Eggs are pelagic (most at 10-90m depth), with movement limited to currents in spawning locations (Smith, Sibunka, and Wells 1975). Larvae move from the surface down to about 20 m, with larger larvae making longer migrations. This habit may assist in the dispersal of larvae because circulation at the surface transports the larvae further and is likely associated with feeding behavior (Smith, Sibunka, and Wells 1978). Larvae feed on zooplankton, particularly Calanus (J. Kittel 2025). Once larvae reach metamorphosis size (11.6-16 mm) bottom temperature (4-8°C) and depth (41-70m) are the most important parameters for determining settlement (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). Juveniles utilize marine and estuarine sandy and muddy substrates, as well as estuarine and marine water columns (Collette and Klein-MacPhee 2002). Rotifers, copepods, and brine shrimp nauplii were fed to juveniles in laboratory studies (Benoit and Pepin 1999). Calanus copepods are considered a critical food source for larvae/juveniles (Purchase, Boyce, and Brown 2000). Amphipods (41% by weight) and polychaetes (38.5%) dominate the diet of adult yellowtail, but they also can consume cnidarians, small crabs and shrimps, mysids, cumaceans, isopods, univalve and bivalve mollusks, echinoderms, and sometimes small fish (Bowman et al. 1987) (Bowman et al. 2000). Similar to other groundfish, yellowtail may be impacted by increasing grey seal and dogfish populations and resulting increased predation pressures (J. Kittel 2025). Other predators of juvenile and adult yellowtail include spiny dogfish, skates, cod, hakes, and monkfish (Johnson et al. 1999). Minimal migration patterns show that Southern New England yellowtail traveled eastward during spring and summer and westward in the fall and winter as a result of seasonal changes in temperature (Smith, Sibunka, and Wells 1975). Recent tagging studies, however, show that individuals generally don’t move between stocks (Goethel, Legault, and Cadrin 2015). Yellowtail flounder are managed by the New England Fishery Management Council through the Northeast Multispecies (Groundfish) Fishery Management Plan. The Georges Bank stock is jointly managed by the Council and Canada, as the stock extends beyond the international boundary. The Cape Cod/Gulf of Maine stock is not overfished and not subject to overfishing (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025a). The Georges Bank stock is overfished but under a rebuilding plan; overfishing status is unknown (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025b). The Southern New England/Mid-Atlantic stock is overfished but under a rebuilding plan, but not subject to overfishing (Northeast Fisheries Science Center (NEFSC) 2025c).
